docs.title: Front-End Development, from zero
docs.text:
Salaam, and welcome. This is a free course that takes you from never having written code to building real websites that you can put online and show the world.

You do not need any experience. You do not need to pay. You do not need to keep up with a class. You start when you want, and you move at your own speed.

docs.title: What this book is
docs.text:
This is the full written course for Front-End Development at Bano Qabil Sahiwal. It teaches you to build modern websites with HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Tailwind, React, and more. By the end you will have a real ecommerce project and a personal portfolio that you can show to an employer or a client.

The book stands on its own. If you attend the in-person Bano Qabil Sahiwal class, this is your textbook. If you are studying from home in another city, you can finish the whole thing without ever sitting in a classroom.

docs.title: How to use this book
docs.text:
• Go in order. Each chapter builds on the one before it.
  • Type the code yourself. Do not copy and paste. Your hands need the practice.
  • Do the "Try this" steps. Reading is not enough. You learn by doing.
  • Get stuck, then get unstuck. Being stuck is normal. Every lesson shows you how to find your way out.
  • Take your time. There is no deadline. A lesson a day is great progress.

Hard words get a dotted underline. Hover or tap them to see a short meaning, in English and Urdu where it helps.

docs.title: What you'll be able to build
docs.text:
• A personal portfolio website
  • A working ecommerce store with a cart and checkout
  • Animated, modern pages that feel alive
  • Sites you can deploy to a live URL for free

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
1. A laptop or computer with Windows, macOS, or Linux.
  2. An internet connection.
  3. A free Google account and a free GitHub account (we set these up together).

That is all. Every tool in this course is free.

docs.title: Marking lessons complete
docs.text: Soon you will be able to sign in and mark each lesson complete, so you can track your progress across the whole course. That feature is on the way. For now, just keep a simple note of where you stopped.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
You are at the very beginning, which is the perfect place to be.

Start with lesson 0.1 →

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Every technical term used in this book, in plain English with an Urdu note where it helps. Terms are added here as each lesson is written. Use the search box to jump to a term, or browse A to Z below.

docs.title: active learning
docs.text:
Learning by doing the thing yourself, not just watching or reading. First introduced in lesson 0.4.

Roman Urdu: khud kar ke seekhna

docs.title: active recall
docs.text:
Pulling an answer from memory instead of re-reading it. It builds strong memory. First introduced in lesson 0.4.

Roman Urdu: yaad se jawab nikalna, dobara parhne ke bajaye

docs.title: absolute URL
docs.text:
A full web address that starts with https and points to another website. First introduced in lesson 2.2.

Roman Urdu: poora pata jo https se shuru hota hai

docs.title: accessibility
docs.text:
Making a page usable for everyone, including people with disabilities. First introduced in lesson 2.4.

Roman Urdu: safhe ko sab ke liye qabil-e-istemal banana

docs.title: alt text
docs.text:
Text that describes an image for screen readers, search engines, and broken images. First introduced in lesson 2.2.

Roman Urdu: tasveer ki tafseel wala likha

docs.title: anchor
docs.text:
The <a> tag that creates a link to another page or site. First introduced in lesson 2.2.

Roman Urdu: link banane wala tag

docs.title: attribute
docs.text:
Extra information inside an opening tag, written as name="value". First introduced in lesson 2.1.

Roman Urdu: tag ke andar di gayi maloomat

docs.title: align-items
docs.text:
A flexbox property that aligns items along the cross axis. First introduced in lesson 3.2.

Roman Urdu: items ko cross axis par set karta hai

docs.title: @apply
docs.text:
A CSS rule that puts several Tailwind utilities into one custom class. First introduced in lesson 5.2.

Roman Urdu: kai Tailwind classes ko ek class mein rakhne ka tareeqa

docs.title: arbitrary value
docs.text:
A custom Tailwind value written inside square brackets, like bg-[#0fab95]. First introduced in lesson 5.3.

Roman Urdu: square brackets mein likhi gayi custom Tailwind value

docs.title: AbortController
docs.text:
A browser tool that can cancel a running fetch request. First introduced in lesson 11.2.

Roman Urdu: chalti hui fetch ko rokne wala tool

docs.title: API
docs.text:
A web address that returns data your app can use, usually as JSON. First introduced in lesson 11.2.

Roman Urdu: aik web address jo data wapas karta hai

docs.title: argument
docs.text:
The real value you pass when you call a function. First introduced in lesson 7.3.

Roman Urdu: asal value jo aap function call karte waqt dete hain

docs.title: arrow function
docs.text:
A short way to write a function using the => arrow. First introduced in lesson 7.3.

Roman Urdu: function likhne ka chhota tareeqa jo => arrow se hota hai

docs.title: App Router
docs.text:
The Next.js routing system that uses the app folder, where folders become routes. First introduced in lesson 15.1.

Roman Urdu: app folder wala routing system

docs.title: AnimatePresence
docs.text:
A Framer Motion wrapper that lets a component play an exit animation before it unmounts. First introduced in lesson 16.1.

Roman Urdu: jane se pehle exit animation chalane wala wrapper

docs.title: AmbientLight
docs.text:
A Three.js light that lights everything evenly with no shadows. First introduced in lesson 17.2.

Roman Urdu: har taraf se halki roshni

docs.title: AnimationMixer
docs.text:
A Three.js object that controls playback of a model's animation clips. First introduced in lesson 17.3.

Roman Urdu: model ki animation chalane wala

docs.title: animation budget
docs.text:
The small limit of motion a page should use before it feels heavy or slow. First introduced in lesson 16.4.

Roman Urdu: ek page par jaayaz haraka ki hadd

docs.title: Anime.js
docs.text:
A small, free JavaScript animation library, good for self-contained effects and SVG drawing. First introduced in lesson 16.2.

Roman Urdu: chhoti animation library

docs.title: analytics
docs.text:
Data about who visits your site and which pages they see. First introduced in lesson 19.4.

Roman Urdu: maaloomat ke kitne log aaye aur kaun se pages dekhe

docs.title: any
docs.text:
A TypeScript type that turns off all checks for a value. Avoid it. First introduced in lesson 18.1.

Roman Urdu: saare checks band kar dene wali type

docs.title: assertion
docs.text:
A test line that checks if a value matches what you expect. First introduced in lesson 18.3.

Roman Urdu: aik line jo value ko expected se milati hai

docs.title: array
docs.text:
An ordered list of values stored together. First introduced in lesson 8.1.

Roman Urdu: cheezon ki list

docs.title: backend
docs.text:
The part of a website that runs on a server and handles data, accounts, and logic. Users never see it. First introduced in lesson 1.1.

Roman Urdu: website ka woh hissa jo server par chalta hai aur data sambhalta hai

docs.title: browser
docs.text:
A program that turns website code into the page you see, like Chrome or Firefox. First introduced in lesson 0.1.

Roman Urdu: woh app jis se tum websites dekhte ho

docs.title: block element
docs.text:
An element that starts on a new line and takes the full width, like a paragraph. First introduced in lesson 2.2.

Roman Urdu: nayi line se shuru hone wala element

docs.title: body
docs.text:
The part of an HTML page that holds the content users see on screen. First introduced in lesson 2.1.

Roman Urdu: page ka wo hissa jo user screen par dekhta hai

docs.title: box model
docs.text:
The idea that every element is a box of content, padding, border, and margin. First introduced in lesson 3.1.

Roman Urdu: har element ek dabba hai

docs.title: breakpoint
docs.text:
The screen width where your responsive layout changes. First introduced in lesson 3.4.

Roman Urdu: woh width jahan layout badalta hai

docs.title: branch
docs.text:
A separate line of work in your repo where you can try changes safely. First introduced in lesson 6.3.

Roman Urdu: alag line jahan aap mehfooz tareeqe se naya kaam karte hain

docs.title: boolean
docs.text:
A value that is only true or false. First introduced in lesson 7.1.

Roman Urdu: aisi value jo sirf true ya false hoti hai

docs.title: build
docs.text:
A step that turns your source files into the final files the browser needs. First introduced in lesson 9.1.

Roman Urdu: source files ko final files mein badalne ka step

docs.title: BrowserRouter
docs.text:
The React Router wrapper that turns on routing for the whole app. First introduced in lesson 13.1.

Roman Urdu: poori app mein routing chalu karne wala wrapper

docs.title: bundle
docs.text:
The single packed file of all your JavaScript that the browser downloads. First introduced in lesson 19.3.

Roman Urdu: tamam JavaScript ka ek packed file

docs.title: boundary
docs.text:
In Next.js, the point in the tree where server code switches to client code. First introduced in lesson 15.2.

Roman Urdu: woh jagah jahan server se client shuru hota hai

docs.title: command
docs.text:
One typed instruction you give the shell, like ls or cd. First introduced in lesson 1.3.

Roman Urdu: ek likhi hidayat jo tum shell ko dete ho

docs.title: Console
docs.text:
The DevTools tab that shows errors and runs small bits of JavaScript. First introduced in lesson 1.4.

Roman Urdu: woh tab jahan errors dikhte aur JavaScript chalti hai

docs.title: cell
docs.text:
One box in a spreadsheet, named by its column and row, like B2. First introduced in lesson 0.2.

Roman Urdu: spreadsheet ka ek khaana

docs.title: case study
docs.text:
A short writeup of one project, covering the problem, your choices, and what you learned. First introduced in lesson 20.1.

Roman Urdu: ek project ki chhoti kahani aur uska tajurba

docs.title: cascade
docs.text:
How later and stronger rules win when CSS styles clash. First introduced in lesson 3.1.

Roman Urdu: baad wale aur mazboot rule jeet jate hain

docs.title: class
docs.text:
A reusable label you put on many elements, targeted in CSS with a dot. First introduced in lesson 3.1.

Roman Urdu: dobara use hone wala naam

docs.title: clamp
docs.text:
A CSS function that keeps a size between a minimum and a maximum. First introduced in lesson 3.4.

Roman Urdu: aisa function jo size ko kam se kam aur zyada se zyada ke beech rakhta hai

docs.title: cross axis
docs.text:
In flexbox, the direction across the main axis, at ninety degrees. First introduced in lesson 3.2.

Roman Urdu: main axis ke aaray chalne wali simat

docs.title: CSS
docs.text:
Cascading Style Sheets, the language that styles your HTML. First introduced in lesson 3.1.

Roman Urdu: page ko style karne wali language

docs.title: CSS Grid
docs.text:
A layout system that places items in rows and columns at the same time. First introduced in lesson 3.3.

Roman Urdu: rows aur columns dono mein layout banane ka tareeqa

docs.title: context
docs.text:
A shared box of data that many components can read without props. First introduced in lesson 13.3.

Roman Urdu: sab pages ke liye shared data

docs.title: camera
docs.text:
In 3D, your viewpoint into the scene that decides what you see. First introduced in lesson 17.1.

Roman Urdu: aap ka nuqta nazar, kahan se dekh rahe hain

docs.title: Canvas
docs.text:
The React Three Fiber component that creates the scene, camera, and renderer and holds your 3D objects. First introduced in lesson 17.4.

Roman Urdu: R3F ka component jo 3D scene khud bana deta hai

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text:
Services that run checks and deploy your code automatically. First introduced in lesson 18.4.

Roman Urdu: check aur deploy khud karne wali services

docs.title: commit hook
docs.text:
A command that runs automatically before a Git commit. First introduced in lesson 18.4.

Roman Urdu: commit se pehle khud chalne wala command

docs.title: component library
docs.text:
A package of ready-made UI parts like buttons and menus. First introduced in lesson 18.2.

Roman Urdu: tayar shuda UI hisson ka package

docs.title: coverage
docs.text:
A number showing how much of your code the tests run. First introduced in lesson 18.3.

Roman Urdu: kitna code tests chalate hain, uska number

docs.title: CSS variable
docs.text:
A named value in CSS you set once and reuse, like a theme color. First introduced in lesson 18.2.

Roman Urdu: CSS mein ek naam wali value jo baar baar use hoti hai

docs.title: CLS
docs.text:
Cumulative Layout Shift, how much the page jumps around while loading. First introduced in lesson 19.3.

Roman Urdu: page load hote waqt kitna uchalta hai

docs.title: code splitting
docs.text:
Loading only the JavaScript a page needs now, and the rest later. First introduced in lesson 19.3.

Roman Urdu: sirf zaroori JavaScript abhi load karna

docs.title: Core Web Vitals
docs.text:
Three numbers Google uses to judge real page experience: LCP, INP, and CLS. First introduced in lesson 19.3.

Roman Urdu: teen ahem maap jo page ka tajurba naapte hain

docs.title: consumer
docs.text:
Any component that reads the value from a Context. First introduced in lesson 14.3.

Roman Urdu: jo component Context ki value parhta hai

docs.title: caching
docs.text:
Saving a fetch result so Next.js can reuse it and load faster. First introduced in lesson 15.3.

Roman Urdu: fetch ka result save rakhna taake dobara tezi se mile

docs.title: client component
docs.text:
A Next.js component that runs in the browser and can use state, effects, and clicks. First introduced in lesson 15.2.

Roman Urdu: browser mein chalne wala interactive component

docs.title: createContext
docs.text:
The React function that creates a Context object. First introduced in lesson 14.3.

Roman Urdu: Context banane wala function

docs.title: call to action
docs.text:
The main button or link you want people to click on a page. First introduced in lesson 5.4.

Roman Urdu: asal button jo dabwana ho

docs.title: card
docs.text:
A rounded box that groups related content with a shadow and padding. First introduced in lesson 5.2.

Roman Urdu: ek box jis mein milti julti cheezen rakhi jati hain

docs.title: CDN
docs.text:
A web link that delivers a file fast from servers near the user. First introduced in lesson 5.1.

Roman Urdu: file ko jaldi pohanchane wala web link

docs.title: client brief
docs.text:
The notes and answers a client gives you about the job. First introduced in lesson 5.4.

Roman Urdu: client ki di hui maaloomat

docs.title: component
docs.text:
A reusable piece of a website, like a card or a button. First introduced in lesson 5.2.

Roman Urdu: website ka dobara istemaal hone wala hissa

docs.title: checkout
docs.text:
A Git command to switch branches, and with -b to create one too. First introduced in lesson 6.3.

Roman Urdu: branch badalne ka command

docs.title: clone
docs.text:
Copying a whole repository from GitHub onto your computer. First introduced in lesson 6.2.

Roman Urdu: poori repo apne computer par copy karna

docs.title: code review
docs.text:
When people read your changes and comment before they merge. First introduced in lesson 6.4.

Roman Urdu: code ka jaiza lena

docs.title: commit
docs.text:
One saved snapshot of your staged changes. First introduced in lesson 6.1.

Roman Urdu: ek saved snapshot

docs.title: commit message
docs.text:
A short note that describes what a commit changed. First introduced in lesson 6.1.

Roman Urdu: commit ki tafseel ka chhota note

docs.title: callback
docs.text:
A function you pass into another function to be called later. First introduced in lesson 7.4.

Roman Urdu: woh function jo aap baad mein chalne ke liye bhejte hain

docs.title: closure
docs.text:
A function that remembers variables from where it was made. First introduced in lesson 7.3.

Roman Urdu: aik function jo apni jaye paidaish ke variables yaad rakhta hai

docs.title: conditional
docs.text:
Code that runs only when a condition is true. First introduced in lesson 7.2.

Roman Urdu: shart par chalne wala code

docs.title: const
docs.text:
A keyword for a variable that cannot be reassigned. First introduced in lesson 7.1.

Roman Urdu: aisi variable ke liye jiski value dobara set nahi ki ja sakti

docs.title: classList
docs.text:
An object on an element used to add, remove, toggle, and check CSS classes. First introduced in lesson 8.3.

Roman Urdu: classes add, remove aur toggle karne ke liye

docs.title: createElement
docs.text:
A method that builds a new element in memory before you attach it. First introduced in lesson 8.3.

Roman Urdu: naya element banata hai jise baad mein page par lagaya jata hai

docs.title: cache
docs.text:
Copies of your files kept close to users so the site loads fast. First introduced in lesson 9.1.

Roman Urdu: files ki copies jo users ke kareeb rakhi jati hain

docs.title: cPanel
docs.text:
A control panel with tiles and buttons to manage a website on a server. First introduced in lesson 9.3.

Roman Urdu: website manage karne ka control panel

docs.title: custom domain
docs.text:
Your own web address, like yourname.com, that points to your site. First introduced in lesson 9.4.

Roman Urdu: aap ka apna web address

docs.title: children
docs.text:
A special React prop holding content placed between a component's open and close tags. First introduced in lesson 10.2.

Roman Urdu: tags ke darmiyan rakha gaya content

docs.title: cleanup function
docs.text:
A function you return from an effect to stop or undo it. First introduced in lesson 11.1.

Roman Urdu: effect ko rokne wala function

docs.title: computed property name
docs.text:
An object key written in square brackets so its value becomes the key. First introduced in lesson 11.3.

Roman Urdu: bracket mein likhi gayi key jo value se banti hai

docs.title: conditional rendering
docs.text:
Showing different UI based on data or a condition. First introduced in lesson 10.4.

Roman Urdu: data ke hisaab se alag UI dikhana

docs.title: controlled input
docs.text:
An input whose value comes from state and updates through onChange. First introduced in lesson 10.3.

Roman Urdu: jis input ki value state se aati hai

docs.title: custom hook
docs.text:
A reusable function whose name starts with use and uses hooks inside. First introduced in lesson 11.2.

Roman Urdu: dobara istemal hone wala apna hook

docs.title: DevTools
docs.text:
A set of tools built into the browser for inspecting and editing web pages. First introduced in lesson 1.4.

Roman Urdu: browser mein bana tool jo page ka code dikhata aur badalta hai

docs.title: directory
docs.text:
Another word for a folder, used a lot in the terminal. First introduced in lesson 1.3.

Roman Urdu: folder ka dusra naam

docs.title: DNS
docs.text:
The internet's phone book. It turns a name like google.com into a number address. First introduced in lesson 0.1.

Roman Urdu: internet ki phone book jo naam ko address mein badalti hai

docs.title: div
docs.text:
A plain box tag with no meaning, used for grouping when no semantic tag fits. First introduced in lesson 2.4.

Roman Urdu: bina matlab wala dabba tag

docs.title: data model
docs.text:
The fixed shape and keys that each piece of data follows. First introduced in lesson 13.1.

Roman Urdu: har data ki tay-shuda shakal aur keys

docs.title: derived state
docs.text:
Data you compute from other state during render, not stored separately. First introduced in lesson 13.2.

Roman Urdu: doosri state se banaya gaya data jo store nahi hota

docs.title: dynamic rendering
docs.text:
Rendering a page fresh for each request instead of once at build time. First introduced in lesson 15.1.

Roman Urdu: har request par naya banna

docs.title: DirectionalLight
docs.text:
A Three.js light with parallel rays from one direction, like the sun. First introduced in lesson 17.2.

Roman Urdu: sooraj jaisi seedhi roshni

docs.title: DRACO
docs.text:
A method to compress 3D model geometry so files get much smaller. First introduced in lesson 17.3.

Roman Urdu: model ko chota karne ka tareeqa

docs.title: drei
docs.text:
A helper pack for React Three Fiber with ready-made tools like OrbitControls and Environment. First introduced in lesson 17.4.

Roman Urdu: R3F ke liye tayar helpers ka package

docs.title: demo
docs.text:
A short walkthrough that shows your app working. First introduced in lesson 20.3.

Roman Urdu: chhoti video jo app chalti hui dikhaye

docs.title: deposit
docs.text:
Money the client pays before you start, often half the price. First introduced in lesson 20.4.

Roman Urdu: pehle se di gayi raqam

docs.title: declarative animation
docs.text:
A style where you state the end result and the library fills in the frames. First introduced in lesson 16.2.

Roman Urdu: aap natija batate hain, library kaam karti hai

docs.title: debounce
docs.text:
Waiting until a value stops changing for a short delay before acting on it. First introduced in lesson 14.2.

Roman Urdu: thora intezar karna jab tak typing ruk na jaye

docs.title: devDependencies
docs.text:
Tools needed only while building a project, not by real users. First introduced in lesson 18.4.

Roman Urdu: sirf banane ke waqt darkar tools

docs.title: Dev Mode
docs.text:
A Figma view that shows exact values and CSS for developers. First introduced in lesson 5.3.

Roman Urdu: Figma ka woh hissa jo developer ke liye values dikhata hai

docs.title: deploy
docs.text:
To put your site or app on a server so people can use it live. First introduced in lesson 6.4.

Roman Urdu: site ko live karna

docs.title: destructuring
docs.text:
Pulling values out of an array or object into separate variables in one line. First introduced in lesson 8.2.

Roman Urdu: array ya object se values nikalna

docs.title: DOM
docs.text:
The Document Object Model. The browser's live tree of objects made from your HTML. First introduced in lesson 8.3.

Roman Urdu: HTML ka live tree jise JavaScript parh aur badal sakta hai

docs.title: DNS record
docs.text:
An entry that tells the internet where your domain should point. First introduced in lesson 9.4.

Roman Urdu: domain ka pata batane wali entry

docs.title: dev server
docs.text:
The local server that shows your app while you build it. First introduced in lesson 10.1.

Roman Urdu: local server jo banate waqt app dikhata hai

docs.title: dependency array
docs.text:
The list of values that tells React when to run an effect again. First introduced in lesson 11.1.

Roman Urdu: woh values jin par effect dobara chalta hai

docs.title: duration
docs.text:
How long a tween runs, given in seconds. First introduced in lesson 12.1.

Roman Urdu: animation kitni der chale, second mein

docs.title: element
docs.text:
An opening tag, its content, and its closing tag taken together. First introduced in lesson 2.1.

Roman Urdu: opening tag, content aur closing tag mila kar ek element

docs.title: Elements panel
docs.text:
The DevTools tab that shows a page's HTML and CSS, where you can live-edit both. First introduced in lesson 1.4.

Roman Urdu: woh tab jahan page ka HTML aur CSS dekh kar badal sakte ho

docs.title: extension (file)
docs.text:
The short part after the dot in a file name. It tells the computer the file type. First introduced in lesson 0.1.

Roman Urdu: file ke naam ke baad ka chhota hissa jo type batata hai

docs.title: export
docs.text:
Saving an icon or image from Figma as a file on your computer. First introduced in lesson 5.3.

Roman Urdu: icon ya image ko file ke taur par save karna

docs.title: error
docs.text:
An object that describes what went wrong while code ran. First introduced in lesson 7.4.

Roman Urdu: ek object jo batata hai kya ghalat hua

docs.title: ES2015
docs.text:
The 2015 version of JavaScript, also called ES6, that added modern syntax. First introduced in lesson 8.2.

Roman Urdu: JavaScript ka 2015 wala modern version

docs.title: event
docs.text:
Something that happens on a page, like a click or a keystroke. First introduced in lesson 8.4.

Roman Urdu: aisa kaam jo page par hota hai, jaise click

docs.title: event delegation
docs.text:
Attaching one listener to a parent to handle events from its children. First introduced in lesson 8.4.

Roman Urdu: parent par aik listener laga kar bachon ko sambhalna

docs.title: event listener
docs.text:
Code that waits for an event and runs a function when it happens. First introduced in lesson 8.4.

Roman Urdu: code jo kisi event ka intezaar karta hai

docs.title: event object
docs.text:
An object the browser passes to your handler with details about the event. First introduced in lesson 8.4.

Roman Urdu: event ki tafseel rakhne wala object

docs.title: environment variable
docs.text:
A secret value you set in the host's dashboard, not in your code. First introduced in lesson 9.2.

Roman Urdu: aik secret value jo aap dashboard mein set karte hain, code mein nahi

docs.title: event handler
docs.text:
A function that runs when an event like a click or submit happens. First introduced in lesson 10.3.

Roman Urdu: event hone par chalne wala function

docs.title: ease
docs.text:
The speed curve of a tween, how it speeds up and slows down. First introduced in lesson 12.1.

Roman Urdu: animation ki raftaar ka curve

docs.title: easing
docs.text:
A function that shapes the speed of motion over time. First introduced in lesson 16.3.

Roman Urdu: function jo waqt ke sath raftaar ki shakl banata hai

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
A drei helper that adds soft lighting and reflections from a preset. First introduced in lesson 17.4.

Roman Urdu: preset se aasan, naram lighting deta hai

docs.title: ESLint
docs.text:
A tool that finds problems in your code. First introduced in lesson 18.4.

Roman Urdu: code ke masail dhoondne wala tool

docs.title: error monitoring
docs.text:
A tool that catches errors on real users' devices and reports them to you. First introduced in lesson 19.4.

Roman Urdu: aisa tool jo asli users ke errors pakar kar aap ko batata hai

docs.title: end
docs.text:
In ScrollTrigger, the scroll position where the trigger finishes. First introduced in lesson 12.2.

Roman Urdu: jahan animation khatam hoti hai

docs.title: frontend
docs.text:
The part of a website users see and click, built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript in the browser. First introduced in lesson 1.1.

Roman Urdu: website ka woh hissa jo browser mein dikhta aur click hota hai

docs.title: full-stack
docs.text:
A developer who builds both the frontend and the backend of a website. First introduced in lesson 1.1.

Roman Urdu: woh developer jo frontend aur backend dono banata hai

docs.title: file
docs.text:
One saved thing on a computer, like a photo or a note. First introduced in lesson 0.1.

Roman Urdu: computer par save ek cheez

docs.title: folder
docs.text:
A container that holds files and other folders. First introduced in lesson 0.1.

Roman Urdu: ek dabba jis mein files rakhi jati hain

docs.title: formula
docs.text:
An instruction in a spreadsheet cell that calculates a result. It starts with an equals sign. First introduced in lesson 0.2.

docs.text:

A file format that looks the same on every device. Good for sending finished work. First introduced in lesson 0.2.

A service to receive payments from abroad, then withdraw to your local bank. First introduced in lesson 20.4.

docs.title: pinned repo

A repo you fix to the top of your GitHub profile as featured work. First introduced in lesson 20.3.

A personal website that shows your projects and skills to employers. First introduced in lesson 20.1.

A tool that formats your code automatically. First introduced in lesson 18.4.

docs.title: pin

Space inside the border, between content and the edge. First introduced in lesson 3.1.

Physically based rendering, a realistic way to show light on 3D surfaces. First introduced in lesson 17.2.

docs.title: poly count

In Next.js, a page.js or page.jsx file that defines the page shown for its route. First introduced in lesson 15.1.

docs.title: product card

A component where the same props always give the same output, with no side effects. First introduced in lesson 10.2.

docs.title: queryKey

docs.title: relative URL

docs.title: required
docs.text:

One page that changes its layout to fit any screen size. First introduced in lesson 3.4.

A Tailwind prefix like md: that applies a class only at that screen width and up. First introduced in lesson 5.1.

The online copy of your repository that your local Git connects to. First introduced in lesson 6.2.

An unstyled library that gives accessible component behavior with no looks. First introduced in lesson 18.2.

docs.text:
An email service that sends real email from your code, with a free tier. First introduced in lesson 20.2.

docs.text:
A tool to render components and interact with them like a user. First introduced in lesson 18.3.

docs.text:
The folder where Git stores your saved history. First introduced in lesson 6.1.

docs.title: return

HTML where each tag describes the meaning of its content, not just its look. First introduced in lesson 2.4.

Your set of brand colours and values that Tailwind builds classes from. First introduced in lesson 5.2.

A popular kind of Linux. It is what WSL2 installs by default. First introduced in lesson 1.2.

A TanStack Query hook that fetches and caches data, giving you data, isLoading, and isError. First introduced in lesson 19.2.

A test that checks one small piece of logic on its own. First introduced in lesson 18.3.

docs.text:
A custom hook that keeps React state in sync with the browser's localStorage. First introduced in lesson 14.2.

docs.title: useMediaQuery
docs.text:
A custom hook that returns true or false for a CSS media query in JavaScript. First introduced in lesson 14.2.

docs.title: Vitest
docs.text:
A fast test runner that finds and runs your test files. First introduced in lesson 18.3.

docs.text:
A system that saves snapshots of your project over time. First introduced in lesson 6.1.

Roman Urdu: aap ke project ke purane versions save karne ka system

docs.title: variable
docs.text:
A named box that holds a value. First introduced in lesson 7.1.

Roman Urdu: aik naam wala dabba jo koi value rakhta hai

docs.title: validation
docs.text:
Checking that user input is correct before you use it. First introduced in lesson 8.4.

Roman Urdu: input sahi hai ya nahi, yeh jaanchna

docs.title: Vercel
docs.text:
A modern host that connects to GitHub and deploys frontend sites. First introduced in lesson 9.2.

Roman Urdu: aik modern host jo GitHub se jurta hai aur site live karta hai

docs.title: WSL2
docs.text:
Windows Subsystem for Linux v2. It runs a real Linux system inside Windows. First introduced in lesson 1.2.

Roman Urdu: Windows ke andar Linux chalane ka tareeqa

docs.title: workflow
docs.text:
A set order of steps you follow to finish a job. First introduced in lesson 5.4.

Roman Urdu: kaam karne ka tareeqa

docs.title: whileHover
docs.text:
A Framer Motion gesture prop that animates an element while the mouse sits over it. First introduced in lesson 16.1.

Roman Urdu: hover ke dauran animate karne wala prop

docs.title: WebGL
docs.text:
Browser tech that talks to the GPU to draw 2D and 3D graphics fast. First introduced in lesson 17.1.

Roman Urdu: browser ka graphics engine jo GPU se baat karta hai

docs.title: working directory
docs.text:
Your project folder with the files you edit. First introduced in lesson 6.1.

Roman Urdu: aap ka project folder jahan files edit hoti hain

docs.title: Zustand
docs.text:
A tiny state management library for React. First introduced in lesson 19.1.

Roman Urdu: chhoti aur asaan state library

docs.title: Chapter 0: Getting Ready
docs.text:
Muhammad Umar Sajid · Trainer at Bano Qabil Sahiwal · Front End Development and Graphic Design

Before we write any code, let us get comfortable with the computer, the internet, and a few habits that make learning easier.

If you have used a phone but barely touched a laptop, this chapter is for you. Nothing here is hard. Take it slow.

docs.title: Lessons in this chapter
docs.text:
• 0.1 Your computer and the internet: files, folders, browsers, and what happens when you visit a website.
  • 0.2 Word, Excel, and Google for developers: everyday tools, and how to search the way developers do.
  • 0.3 Using AI to learn: ChatGPT and Claude as a patient study partner, used honestly.
  • 0.4 Learning to learn: simple habits that make hard things easier.

No rush

There is no clock and no deadline. Finish a lesson, take a break, and come back when you are ready. The lessons will wait for you.

docs.title: Mega assignment
docs.text:
Set up your developer identity and learning system

Before you write a single line of code, real developers set up the accounts and habits they will use for years. Do it now, properly, once.

What you build

  • A professional email and a GitHub account that share the same real name and a clean username (something like firstname-lastname, not xXgamer786Xx), with a real photo and a one-line bio.
  • A learning journal: a Google Doc or Sheet where, after every lesson, you write the date, one line on what you learned, and one thing that confused you.
  • A weekly study plan. Self-paced does not mean no plan. Pick your days and times and write them down where you will see them.

Done when

  • Your GitHub username looks like something you would put on a CV.
  • Your GitHub and email use the same real name.
  • Your learning journal has its first entry, dated today.
  • Your weekly study plan is written down somewhere visible.

Stretch goal: Write a short "why I am learning this" paragraph at the top of your journal. On the hard days, you will read it.

Start lesson 0.1 →

docs.title: 0.1 Your Computer & The Internet
docs.text:
Before you write a single line of code, you need to be at home on your computer and your keyboard. This lesson is the ground floor. By the end you can type with more confidence, find and name your files, recognise the common file types you will meet, open a web browser, and explain in plain words how the internet brings a web page to your screen.

(Roman Urdu: Is sabaq ke baad tum behtar type kar sako ge, apni files dhoond aur naam de sako ge, aam file types pehchaan sako ge, browser khol sako ge, aur bata sako ge ke internet kaise ek page tumhari screen tak laata hai.)

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• Why typing well matters, and where to practise your speed
  • What a file and a folder are
  • What a file extension is, and the common ones like .jpg, .png, and .pdf
  • What a web browser is
  • How the web works, from the address you type to the page you see

docs.title: Start with your keyboard
docs.text:
You are going to spend years typing. So the first skill, before any code, is typing without staring at the keys. This feels slow now and it pays you back every single day later.

Three simple habits build it:

  1. Keep both hands on the home row (Roman Urdu: keyboard ke beech wali line jahan ungliyan aaram se rehti hain). That is a s d f for the left hand and j k l ; for the right.
  2. Look at the screen, not at your fingers. It is harder at first and worth it.
  3. Go for accuracy first. Speed comes on its own once your fingers know the keys.

You do not need to be fast to code. You need to be steady. A comfortable 30 to 40 words per minute (Roman Urdu: ek minute mein kitne lafz type karte ho) with few mistakes is plenty to begin.

A few minutes a day is enough. These free sites teach you and measure your speed:

  • TypingClub teaches you from zero, one key at a time. Best if you are brand new.
  • Monkeytype and TypingTest.com measure your words per minute and accuracy. Good once you want to get faster.

Five minutes a day

Do one short typing drill before each study session. In a few weeks you will stop hunting for keys, and writing code will feel much smoother.

docs.title: Files and folders
docs.text:
A file (Roman Urdu: ek cheez jo computer par save hoti hai, jaise ek photo ya note) is one saved thing. A photo is a file. A song is a file. The code you write will be a file too.

A folder (Roman Urdu: ek dabba jis mein files rakhi jati hain) holds files. You put files inside folders so you can find them later. A folder can also hold other folders.

Think of it like a cupboard. The cupboard is the folder. The clothes inside are the files.

Make your first folders now

On your computer, open the Desktop. Right click, choose New > Folder, and name it banoqabil. Open it, and make one more folder inside called chapter-0. You will keep your work here.

docs.title: File extensions
docs.text:
Every file name has two parts. The name, then a dot, then a short word. That short word is the extension (Roman Urdu: file ke naam ke baad ka chhota hissa jo batata hai file kis type ki hai). It tells the computer what kind of file this is and which program should open it.

Look at photo.jpg. The name is photo. The extension is jpg, so the computer knows it is a picture. Change the extension and you change how the computer treats the file.

Here are the common ones you will meet again and again:

Type Extensions What they hold Documents .txt, .docx, .pdf plain text, Word documents, fixed-layout files for sharing Images .jpg, .png, .gif, .svg, .webp photos, graphics, logos, simple animations Audio and video .mp3, .mp4 sound and video Code (you will write these) .html, .css, .js web pages, their styling, and their behaviour Bundles .zip many files squeezed into one, to send or store

A few of these are worth knowing now:

  • .jpg is small and great for photos. .png keeps sharp edges and can have a see-through background, so it suits logos and screenshots.
  • .svg is special. It is a picture made of code, so it stays sharp at any size, from a tiny icon to a billboard. You will make these later in the course.
  • .html, .css, and .js are the three files behind almost every website. You will write thousands of them.

Turn on file extensions

Windows hides extensions by default. Open any folder, click View, and turn on File name extensions. Now you can see the .html part. You need this for the whole course.

docs.title: What is a browser?
docs.text:
A browser (Roman Urdu: woh app jisse tum websites dekhte ho, jaise Chrome ya Firefox) is the program you use to open websites. Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Safari are all browsers.

When you build a website, you will open your file in a browser to see it. The browser reads your code and shows the page.

We will use Google Chrome in this course because it is free and works the same on most computers.

docs.title: How the web works
docs.text:
Here is a simple picture. You do not need every detail today, just the shape of it. You type an address, called a URL (Roman Urdu: website ka pata, jaise banoqabil.org), and a page comes back.

A browser window with an address bar holding a URL, and the four-step journey of a web request: you type the address, DNS turns the name into a number, the server sends the site files, and your browser draws the page. https://banoqabil.org The bar at the top is the address bar. The web address (URL) goes there. 1. You type banoqabil.org 2. DNS name to number 3. Server sends the files 4. Browser draws the page From the address you type to the page you see, the whole trip takes under a second.

You type a URL in the address bar. DNS turns that name into a number address, the server at that address sends back your files, and the browser draws the page. All in under a second.

Step by step, here is what happens:

  1. You type an address like banoqabil.org into your browser and press Enter.
  2. Your browser asks, "Where does this live?" A kind of phone book called DNS (Roman Urdu: internet ki phone book jo naam ko address mein badalti hai) gives back a number address.
  3. Your browser sends a request to that address. A computer far away, called a server (Roman Urdu: ek bara computer jo websites rakhta hai aur bhejta hai), sends back the page files.
  4. Your browser reads those files and draws the page on your screen.

That whole trip happens in under a second. Every time you open a website, this is what is going on quietly in the background.

tl;dr

Practise typing a little each day. Files hold your work, folders hold your files, and the extension tells the computer what a file is. A browser shows websites. The internet is your browser asking a far-away server for a page, and getting it back.

Urdu mein chhoti wazahat

Pehle typing par thori mehnat karo, roz thora. File ek saved cheez hai, aur folder ek dabba hai jisme files hoti hain. Extension (jaise .jpg ya .html) batata hai file kis type ki hai. Browser woh app hai jisse websites dikhti hain. Aur internet aise kaam karta hai: tum URL likhte ho, DNS us naam ko address mein badalta hai, server files bhejta hai, aur browser page bana deta hai.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: In your chapter-0 folder, make three folders named practice, images, and notes. Inside notes, make a text file called day-one.txt and write one line: what you hope to build by the end of this course. Save it, then close the folder and find it again. That last step is the real exercise. Knowing where your work lives is a skill you will use every single day.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
1. What is the difference between a file and a folder?
  2. In index.html, which part is the extension?
  3. Name one image extension and one code extension.
  4. Name two web browsers.
  5. In one sentence, what does a server do?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You can find your way around your computer and your keyboard now. Next you will learn the everyday office and search tools that working developers lean on more than beginners expect: documents, spreadsheets, and how to search Google like a professional.

Next lesson: 0.2 Word, Excel & Google Search →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • TypingClub free, full typing course from the first key.
  • MDN - How does the internet work?
  • MDN - Dealing with files

docs.title: 0.2.1 Assignment: Research and build your CV
docs.text:
This is your first real assignment. You are not just reading here. You will produce an actual document that you keep, update, and one day send to an employer or a client. That makes this one of the most practical things in the whole course.

By the end of this assignment you will have:

  • A clear understanding of what a CV is and why it matters right now
  • Experience researching something on your own, the way developers actually work
  • A real one-page CV saved as a PDF, ready to share

docs.title: Step 1: Understand what a CV is and why it matters
docs.text:
A CV (Roman Urdu: taaruf naama, yani woh document jo tumhara hunar aur kaam ek jagah pe batata hai) is often the first thing a client or employer sees before they meet you. It is a one-page summary of who you are professionally: your skills, your projects, your education, and your contact details.

Think of it this way. If you apply for a freelance project online, the client will likely receive ten or twenty applications. They will spend about thirty seconds on each one. Your CV is those thirty seconds. A clear, honest, well-laid-out page gets you to the next round. A messy, vague, or dishonest one does not.

Even before you finish this course, having a CV matters:

  • You can apply for internships or part-time work as you learn.
  • You can show it to family and community contacts who might know of work.
  • Building it now means you only have to update it later, not start from scratch when you are busy with real projects.

docs.title: Step 2: Research like a developer
docs.text: Developers solve problems by searching first. No one knows everything. The skill is finding good answers fast. Here is how to research CV formats the same way.

docs.title: Search terms to try
docs.text:
Open Google and try these exact phrases, one at a time:

  • CV format for students Pakistan
  • fresh graduate CV sample 2024
  • software developer CV no experience
  • front-end developer resume beginner

Look at several results for each search. Do not stop at the first one. You are trying to spot patterns: what do the good ones have in common?

docs.title: Places to find real templates
docs.text: Where to look What you will find How to get there Microsoft Create Free, professional Word templates create.microsoft.com/en-us/templates/resumes Canva Visual, easy-to-customise layouts canva.com/resumes/templates Google Docs template gallery Clean, ready-to-use Docs templates Open Google Docs, click "Template gallery" at the top LinkedIn sample CVs Real profiles from real people Search front-end developer Pakistan on LinkedIn and read profiles

docs.title: Judging good vs bad examples
docs.text:
When you look at a CV, ask yourself:

Question Good sign Bad sign Can I read it in thirty seconds? Clear headings, short bullet points Walls of text, tiny font Does it fit on one page? Yes, nothing wasted Two or three pages with filler Are the contact details visible? Name and email at the top Buried in the middle Does it list real, specific skills? "HTML, CSS, JavaScript" "Good communicator, hard worker" Are there project links? GitHub links, live demo links "Projects: various" Is the formatting consistent? Same font, same spacing throughout Mixed fonts, random bolding

Spend about ten minutes on this step. Save or bookmark one or two examples that you think are well done. You will use them as reference.

docs.title: Step 3: Know the sections of a strong beginner CV
docs.text:
A beginner CV does not need to be long. It needs to be clear and honest. Here are the sections that matter, and what to put in each:

Section What to include What to leave out Name and role Your full name, and the role you want: "Front-End Developer" A photo is optional in Pakistan; a headshot is common here but not required Contact Phone, email, city (Sahiwal, Lahore, etc.), and links to GitHub and LinkedIn if you have them Your full home address is not needed Summary Two or three sentences: who you are, what you are learning, and what you want Do not write "I am a hardworking person who loves challenges". Write what you can actually do. Skills Specific tools and languages you actually know: HTML, CSS, Word, Excel, Google Docs, basic internet search Do not list "Microsoft Office" vaguely. List what you have used. Projects Even one project counts. Write the name, one line describing it, and a link if it is online. For now you can write "in progress" or "coming soon". Do not list things you have not built at all. Education Your highest qualification, the institution name, and the year. Include this Bano Qabil course: "Front-End Development, Bano Qabil Sahiwal, 2024 (in progress)". You do not need to list every grade unless they are strong. Experience Any work, even informal. Helping a family business, doing data entry, teaching someone. Be honest about what you did. Do not call yourself a "manager" if you were a helper.

docs.title: Step 4: A note on honesty (sidq)
docs.text:
Keep your CV honest

Never write a skill you do not have. Never inflate a project into something bigger than it was. Do not say you know React if you have only heard of it.

This is not just about being good. It is about being smart. Every claim on your CV can be tested: an interviewer can ask you to write code live, or give you a task on your first day. A CV that claims too much fails you at the worst moment, when you most need to impress.

The honest CV of a beginner who is clearly learning fast is more attractive to a good employer than a dishonest CV that falls apart under one question.

Write what you have. You will have more to write every month.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

CV میں ہمیشہ سچ لکھو۔ جو مہارت ابھی سیکھ رہے ہو، اسے "in progress" لکھو۔ جو پروجیکٹ بنایا ہو، اس کا لنک دو۔ جھوٹی مہارت لکھنے سے انٹرویو میں بہت شرمندگی ہوتی ہے۔ صداقت نہ صرف دینی فرض ہے بلکہ پیشہ ورانہ زندگی میں بھی بہت فائدہ دیتی ہے۔ ایک سچا اور واضح CV ایک بھرا ہوا جھوٹے دعووں والے CV سے ہمیشہ بہتر ہوتا ہے۔

docs.title: Step 5: Build it in Word or Google Docs
docs.text: Now you actually make it. Follow these steps one at a time.

docs.title: Set up the document
docs.text:
1. Open Google Docs or office.com and create a new blank document.
  2. Set the page margins to narrow: in Google Docs go to File > Page setup, set all margins to 1.5 cm or 0.6 inches. In Word go to Layout > Margins > Narrow. This gives you more room on one page.
  3. Choose one clean font for the whole document. Good choices: Calibri, Arial, Georgia, or Roboto (available in Google Docs). Set the base size to 10 or 11 points.

docs.title: Add your sections
docs.text:
Work through the sections from the table in Step 3, in this order:

  1. Your name at the top, large (16 or 18 points) and bold.
  2. The role you want, just below your name, slightly smaller (12 or 13 points).
  3. One line of contact info: phone | email | city | github.com/yourname (if you have one yet).
  4. A short horizontal line (--- in Docs, or Insert > Horizontal rule) to separate the header.
  5. Your two-line summary.
  6. Skills, as a short comma-separated list or a two-column list.
  7. Projects section, even if it just says "coming soon" for now.
  8. Education.
  9. Experience, if any.

docs.title: Keep it to one page
docs.text:
One page is the rule for beginners. If it spills onto a second page:

  • Reduce the font size by half a point.
  • Tighten line spacing: in Google Docs use Format > Line and paragraph spacing > Single.
  • Cut any sentence that repeats something already said.
  • Remove anything you put in to fill space rather than to inform.

docs.title: Make the formatting consistent
docs.text:
Go through the whole document and check:

  • Every section heading is the same size and weight (for example, all bold and 12 points).
  • Every bullet point starts the same way (a verb, or a noun, but not mixed).
  • There is the same amount of space above and below each section.
  • Nothing is underlined unless it is a link. Bold is enough for headings.

docs.title: Export to PDF
docs.text:
When you are satisfied:

  1. In Google Docs: File > Download > PDF Document (.pdf).
  2. In Word: File > Save as > PDF, or File > Export > Create PDF/XPS.
  3. Name the file sensibly: firstname-lastname-cv.pdf, for example ali-hassan-cv.pdf. Lowercase, no spaces, use hyphens. This matters because a recruiter's computer may not display file names with Urdu characters correctly.
  4. Open the PDF and check it. Does it look exactly as you intended? Are all the links clickable?

docs.title: Done when
docs.text:
Go through this checklist before you move on. Tick each item only when it is genuinely true.

  • I looked at at least three CV templates or real examples before I started building
  • My CV fits on exactly one page
  • My name, role, phone, email, and city are at the top
  • Every skill I listed is something I can actually describe or demonstrate
  • The projects section exists, even if it only says "coming soon"
  • My education includes the Bano Qabil course
  • The font and spacing are consistent throughout
  • I exported it as a PDF with the filename firstname-lastname-cv.pdf
  • I opened the PDF and confirmed it looks correct

docs.title: What is next
docs.text:
You have a CV. You will come back and update it many times as you build projects through this course. The next assignment uses Excel or Google Sheets to build something practical: a small system for tracking students, attendance, and results, the kind of thing a real academy actually uses.

Next: 0.2.2 Build an academy system in Excel →

docs.title: 0.2.2 Assignment: Build an academy management system in Excel
docs.text:
Spreadsheets are not just for accountants. Any time you have a list of people, a set of numbers, and a question you want answered automatically, a spreadsheet is the right tool. In this assignment you build a small but real system: a workbook that tracks students, records daily attendance, calculates percentages, flags students who are below the limit, and shows a results summary with pass or fail.

When you finish, you will have a working system that a real Bano Qabil class could actually use. More importantly, you will understand formulas from the inside, not just as things you copy from Google.

By the end you will have:

  • A multi-sheet Excel or Google Sheets workbook with three linked sheets
  • Attendance tracking with automatic percentage calculation
  • IF conditions and conditional formatting to highlight problems
  • A results sheet with marks, percentages, and pass or fail

Open Google Sheets or Excel (at office.com) now and keep it open as you follow along.

docs.title: Step 1: Plan the workbook
docs.text:
Before you touch a cell, think about the structure. A workbook (Roman Urdu: ek file jis mein kai sheets hoti hain, jaise ek kitaab mein kai safhe) is a single file that can hold many sheets. Each sheet is a tab at the bottom.

You will build three sheets:

Sheet name What it holds Students The master list: roll number and student name Attendance Daily attendance for one month: dates across the top, students down the side Summary Each student's attendance percentage, marks, total, percentage, and pass or fail

Right-click each tab at the bottom of your spreadsheet to rename it. Name them exactly as shown: Students, Attendance, Summary. Using consistent names matters because formulas will reference them by name.

docs.title: Step 2: Build the Students sheet
docs.text:
Click the Students tab. Set up two columns:

Cell Type this A1 Roll No B1 Name

Then fill in ten students. It is fine to use made-up names for practice:

A B 1 Ali Hassan 2 Fatima Tariq 3 Usman Raza 4 Zainab Bibi 5 Hamza Malik 6 Amna Khalid 7 Bilal Ahmed 8 Saira Noor 9 Tariq Mehmood 10 Hina Zafar

Put roll numbers in column A (rows 2 to 11) and names in column B (rows 2 to 11). Row 1 is always the header row.

docs.title: Step 3: Build the Attendance sheet
docs.text: Click the Attendance tab.

docs.title: Set up the layout
docs.text:
• Cell A1: type Roll No
  • Cell B1: type Name
  • Starting from C1, type the dates of the month, one per column. For a 20-day class month, you would have C1 through V1. You can just type numbers 1 through 20 for practice.
  • After the last date column, add two more columns with headers:
  • Present (how many days the student attended)
  • Total (how many class days there were)
  • Attendance % (the percentage)
  • Status (whether they are above or below 75%)

A completed header row for a 5-day example looks like:

A B C D E F G H I J Roll No Name 1 2 3 4 5 Present Total Attendance %

For a real month, add more date columns between F and G.

docs.title: Fill in the roll numbers and names
docs.text: In A2 through A11, type the numbers 1 to 10. In B2 through B11, type the same names you put in the Students sheet. (You will learn to pull them automatically from Students in Step 6.)

docs.title: Mark attendance
docs.text:
In each date column, for each student, type either P (present) or A (absent). Use capitals. This is the raw data the formulas will read.

For practice, mark a mix: some students mostly present, and one or two with a lot of absences. That way the conditional formatting in Step 5 will have something to highlight.

docs.title: Step 4: Count attendance and compute the percentage
docs.text: Now the formulas. Stay on the Attendance sheet.

docs.title: Count present days
docs.text:
In the Present column (let us say column H, assuming you have dates in columns C through G for a 5-day example), click cell H2 and type:

=COUNTIF(C2:G2,"P")

What this does: COUNTIF goes through the range C2:G2 and counts every cell that contains exactly "P". The result is the number of days that student was present.

Copy this formula down from H2 to H11 by clicking H2, then dragging the small square at the bottom-right of the cell down through H11. The formula adjusts automatically for each row: H3 becomes =COUNTIF(C3:G3,"P"), and so on.

docs.title: Set the total days
docs.text: In the Total column (column I), type the total number of class days in I2. For a 5-day example, type 5. Then copy this number down through I11. (In a real sheet you could use =COUNTA(C1:G1) to count the date headers automatically, but a plain number is fine for now.)

docs.title: Compute the percentage
docs.text:
In the Attendance % column (column J), click cell J2 and type:

=H2/I2

This divides present days by total days. The result is a decimal, like 0.8. To show it as a percentage, select the J column, then click the % button in the toolbar (or press Ctrl+Shift+5 on Windows). Now 0.8 shows as 80%.

Copy J2 down through J11.

Here is a summary of the three formulas and what they do:

Formula What it does =COUNTIF(C2:G2,"P") Counts how many cells in that row contain the letter P =H2/I2 Divides present days by total days to get the attendance fraction =H2/I2 formatted as % Displays that fraction as a percentage, like 80%

docs.title: Step 5: Flag low attendance with IF and conditional formatting
docs.text: A percentage number is useful. A warning is even more useful because it draws the eye.

docs.title: Add an IF formula for the Status column
docs.text:
In the Status column (column K), click cell K2 and type:

=IF(J2<0.75,"Short","OK")

What this does: IF checks whether J2 is less than 0.75 (which is 75%). If yes, it writes the word Short. If no, it writes OK.

Copy this formula down through K11.

Formula What it does =IF(J2<0.75,"Short","OK") Writes "Short" if attendance is below 75%, otherwise writes "OK"

docs.title: Add conditional formatting to colour low attendance
docs.text:
Conditional formatting (Roman Urdu: agar koi condition poori ho toh cell ka rang badal do) colours a cell automatically based on its value. You will colour any Attendance % cell red when it is below 75%.

In Google Sheets:

  1. Select the range J2:J11 (the Attendance % column).
  2. Go to Format > Conditional formatting.
  3. Under "Format cells if", choose "Less than".
  4. In the value box, type 0.75.
  5. Under "Formatting style", click the fill colour and choose red.
  6. Click Done.

In Excel:

  1. Select J2:J11.
  2. Go to Home > Conditional Formatting > Highlight Cells Rules > Less Than.
  3. Type 0.75 and choose "Light Red Fill" or pick a custom red.
  4. Click OK.

Now any student below 75% attendance will have a red cell that jumps out immediately.

docs.title: Step 6: Pull data from another sheet
docs.text:
You typed the student names again in the Attendance sheet. In a real system you want to pull them from the Students sheet automatically. That way if a name changes, you only fix it in one place.

Click the Attendance sheet, cell B2. Delete what is there and type:

=Students!B2

The ! means "look in this other sheet". Students!B2 means "cell B2 of the Students sheet". Copy this formula down through B11. Now the names in the Attendance sheet come directly from the Students sheet.

Formula pattern What it does =Students!B2 Reads the value from cell B2 on the Students sheet =Students!A2 Reads the roll number from cell A2 on the Students sheet

You can use the same pattern to pull the roll numbers: in A2 of the Attendance sheet, type =Students!A2 and copy it down.

docs.title: Step 7: Build the Summary sheet
docs.text: Click the Summary tab. This sheet brings together attendance and marks in one view.

docs.title: Set up the headers
docs.text: A B C D E F G Roll No Name Attendance % Marks Total Marks Result % Pass / Fail

docs.title: Pull attendance data
docs.text: In A2, type =Students!A2 and copy it down through A11 for roll numbers. In B2, type =Students!B2 and copy it down for names. In C2, type =Attendance!J2 and copy it down. This pulls the attendance percentage straight from the Attendance sheet.

docs.title: Add marks
docs.text: In column D (Marks), type the marks for each student manually. Use numbers out of 100. In column E (Total Marks), type 100 in E2 and copy it down. (Or type any maximum you use.)

docs.title: Calculate result percentage and pass or fail
docs.text:
In F2, type:

=D2/E2

Format column F as a percentage the same way you did before (select the column, click %).

Copy F2 down through F11.

In G2, type:

=IF(F2>=0.5,"Pass","Fail")

This checks whether the result percentage is 50% or more. If yes, it writes Pass. If no, it writes Fail.

Copy G2 down through G11.

Formula What it does =Attendance!J2 Pulls the attendance percentage from the Attendance sheet =D2/E2 Divides marks by total marks to get the result percentage =IF(F2>=0.5,"Pass","Fail") Writes "Pass" if the student scored 50% or more, otherwise "Fail"

Your Summary sheet now updates automatically whenever you change a mark in column D or an attendance entry in the Attendance sheet. That is the real power of a well-built spreadsheet.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

یہ سسٹم تین حصوں میں کام کرتا ہے: Students شیٹ میں طلبا کی فہرست ہے، Attendance شیٹ میں روزانہ حاضری ہے، اور Summary شیٹ میں سب کچھ ایک جگہ دکھتا ہے۔ COUNTIF فارمولا "P" گنتا ہے، IF فارمولا شرط لگاتا ہے جیسے "اگر 75 فیصد سے کم ہو تو Short لکھو"، اور شیٹ کا حوالہ جیسے =Students!B2 ایک شیٹ سے دوسری شیٹ میں ڈیٹا لاتا ہے۔ جب آپ ایک جگہ نمبر بدلتے ہیں تو سب کچھ خود بخود اپڈیٹ ہو جاتا ہے، یہی اسپریڈشیٹ کی اصل طاقت ہے۔

docs.title: Done when
docs.text:
Go through this checklist before moving on. Each item should be genuinely complete.

  • My workbook has three named sheets: Students, Attendance, Summary
  • The Students sheet has roll numbers and names in columns A and B
  • The Attendance sheet has dates across the top and students down the side
  • Each student has a mix of P and A entries in the date columns
  • The Present column uses =COUNTIF(range,"P") and shows a correct count
  • The Attendance % column divides present by total and is formatted as a percentage
  • The Status column uses =IF to write "Short" for below 75% and "OK" otherwise
  • Conditional formatting colours any Attendance % cell below 75% in red
  • The Attendance sheet pulls names from the Students sheet using =Students!B2
  • The Summary sheet pulls attendance percentages from the Attendance sheet using =Attendance!J2
  • The Summary sheet has marks, result percentage, and a Pass/Fail column using =IF
  • Changing one attendance entry in the Attendance sheet updates the Summary sheet

docs.title: What is next
docs.text:
You have built a real, working multi-sheet system. You have used COUNTIF, IF, conditional formatting, and cross-sheet references. These are the same ideas that power far bigger systems used by businesses and schools every day. In the next lesson you will meet a study tool that will help you throughout this whole course: using AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude to learn, without letting them do all the thinking for you.

Next lesson: 0.3 Using AI to learn →

docs.title: 0.2 Word, Excel, and Google for developers
docs.text: Before you write a single line of code, a handful of everyday office tools will save you hours every week. You have heard of all of them. You may not have used them the way a working professional does. Let us fix that, and let us do it for free.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• How to use Word, Excel, and PowerPoint free in your browser, and the Google tools that match them
  • How to write, format, and save a document, and what real documents like contracts are for
  • How to write a first developer CV, honestly
  • How to add up numbers in a spreadsheet, the same way a business runs its accounts
  • How to search Google the way developers do

docs.title: Two free ways to use these tools
docs.text:
You do not need to buy anything. Both Microsoft and Google give you these tools free, inside your browser, with your work saved automatically to the cloud (Roman Urdu: internet par save, jise tum kisi bhi device se khol sakte ho).

  • Microsoft Office on the web. Go to office.com and sign in with a free Microsoft account. You get Word, Excel, and PowerPoint in the browser, with nothing to install. It runs in Microsoft Edge, Chrome, or any browser.
  • Google's tools, which people call Google files. Google Docs for documents, Google Sheets for spreadsheets, and Google Slides for slideshows, the same job as PowerPoint. Sign in with a Google account.

Both save as you type and open from any computer or phone. Pick whichever you like. The ideas are the same in both, so this lesson uses them together.

The job Microsoft Google Documents Word Google Docs Spreadsheets Excel Google Sheets Slideshows PowerPoint Google Slides

docs.title: Documents that mean business
docs.text:
A document is a page of text you can style, save, and send. Beyond simple notes, professionals run real business on documents:

  • Letters, applications, and reports.
  • Contracts (Roman Urdu: likhit samjhauta jise dono taraf maante hain). An employee contract sets the salary, hours, and duties. A company contract sets what each side must deliver, by when, and who is responsible if something goes wrong. Those responsibilities are the liabilities (Roman Urdu: woh zimmedariyan jin ka hisaab dena padta hai).

Writing things down clearly, and keeping a copy, protects everyone. A spoken promise is easy to forget. A written, signed one is not. As a freelancer later, a simple written agreement will save you from many arguments.

To make a document and save it:

  1. Open Google Docs, or office.com for Word, and sign in.
  2. Click the blank page, type a few lines, then select text and make it bold, bigger, or a new colour.
  3. Save a Word copy with File > Download > Microsoft Word (.docx), or a PDF copy for sending.

A .docx file can still be edited. A .pdf looks the same on every phone and laptop, so when you send finished work, send the PDF.

Keep a notes document

Keep a notes document open while you study. Write three lines after each lesson in your own words. Future you will thank present you.

Watch how the pros use it

See how people build real documents and contracts in Word. Search YouTube for creating a contract in Microsoft Word and watch how they set out the terms, the parties, and the signature lines.

docs.title: Make your CV
docs.text:
Your CV (Roman Urdu: tumhara taaruf naama jo tumhara kaam aur hunar batata hai), also called a resume, is the one document that gets you hired. As a front-end developer, a clean one-page CV beats a long fancy one. Include:

  • Your name and the role you want, like "Front-End Developer".
  • Contact: email, phone, and your city.
  • Links that prove your work: your GitHub, your portfolio website, and your LinkedIn.
  • A two-line summary of who you are.
  • Skills: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Tailwind, Git.
  • Projects, two or three, each with one line plus a live link and the code link. For a junior developer, real projects matter more than anything else on the page.
  • Education and courses, including this Bano Qabil course.

Sidq: keep your CV honest

Only put real skills and real projects on your CV. A CV that claims what you cannot do falls apart in the first interview or the first task you are given. Honesty here is not only right, it is the smart long game. Your GitHub and your live links are the proof that backs up every line.

You will build the real, polished version in Chapter 20. For now, a first draft is enough.

Templates and a walkthrough

Start from a free template at Microsoft Create resume templates, and search YouTube for a software developer CV tutorial to see what a strong junior CV looks like.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open a new document and write the first draft of your CV using the list above. Leave the projects section as a heading with the words "coming soon". You will fill it with real projects as you build them through this course. Save it as a PDF so it is ready to share.

docs.title: Spreadsheets: how a business counts its money
docs.text:
A spreadsheet is a grid of boxes called cells. Each cell has an address like A1 or B2. The useful part is that a cell can hold a formula, and the sheet does the maths for you.

A formula always starts with =. That is how the sheet knows you want a calculation, not plain text.

Before the formulas make sense, you need the address system. Columns are letters across the top (A, B, C), rows are numbers down the side (1, 2, 3). So B1 means the box in column B, row 1. When you see a colon, like B1:B3, that is a range: every cell from B1 down to B3. So =SUM(B1:B3) adds three boxes, B1, B2, and B3, together.

Try these in Google Sheets or Excel:

=SUM(B1:B3)             adds the numbers from B1 down to B3
=AVERAGE(B1:B3)         finds the average of those numbers
=COUNTIF(B1:B3,">50")   counts how many of them are greater than 50

This is exactly how a small business runs its money. An accountant tracks every sale and every expense in a sheet, adds up the month's income, subtracts the costs, and sees the profit. A whole company's accounts can live in one well-built spreadsheet: salaries, bills, sales, and totals that update the moment a single number changes. That is the real power, and it starts with the same three formulas you just typed.

Watch how the pros use it

See how accountants and finance professionals run real books in Excel. Search YouTube for Excel for accounting, full course, and keep the Microsoft Excel functions list handy as a reference.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text:
Make a small marks sheet. In column A type three subject names (Maths, English, Urdu) down rows 1 to 3. In column B type a mark out of 100 next to each. Then in cell B4 type =AVERAGE(B1:B3) and press Enter. The sheet gives you the class average at once. Change one mark and watch B4 update on its own. That instant recalculation is the whole point of a spreadsheet.

Did you know

The first spreadsheet program, VisiCalc, came out in 1979. People bought whole computers just to run it. A good idea, clearly explained, sells itself.

docs.title: Search Google like a developer
docs.text:
Most coding problems are already solved somewhere online. The real skill is finding the answer fast. These four tricks do most of the work.

  • Quotes find an exact phrase: "center a div" keeps those words together.
  • site: searches one website only: flexbox site:developer.mozilla.org.
  • Minus removes a word: jaguar speed -car hides the car results.
  • Add the year for fresh answers: best way to center css 2025.

Tip: read more than the first result

The top result is not always the best. Open two or three. Prefer MDN and web.dev. You will meet both many times in this course.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. Which Google tool does the same job as Microsoft Word? And the one for Excel?
  2. What is the difference between a .docx and a PDF, and which do you send?
  3. On a developer's CV, what actually proves your skills are real?
  4. What does every spreadsheet formula start with?
  5. How would you search for "flexbox" only on the MDN website?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You can now write, calculate, and search like a developer, and you have a first CV started. Next you'll meet a study partner that never gets tired of your questions: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude, and how to use them without fooling yourself.

Next: 0.2.1 Build your CV →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Microsoft: Word help and learning a clear official starting point.
  • Microsoft: Excel functions list every function with examples.
  • Google: Refine web searches with operators the full list of search tricks.

docs.title: 0.3 Using AI to learn
docs.text: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude can act like a patient teacher who never gets tired of your questions. Used well, they speed up your learning a lot. Used badly, they do your thinking for you and you learn nothing. This lesson shows you the tools people use worldwide, how to ask them good questions, and the one strict rule that protects your learning while you are a beginner.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What a large language model is, in plain words
  • The big AI tools people use today, and what each one is best at
  • The one strict rule for beginners: ask, do not let it write your code yet
  • How to ask a question that gets a useful answer
  • How to check an answer so you don't learn something wrong
  • Other everyday ways to use AI

docs.title: What an AI chat tool really is
docs.text:
An AI chat tool is a program you talk to in normal language. You type a question, and it writes back an answer. Under the hood is a large language model (Roman Urdu: aisa program jo bohat saara text parh kar agle lafz ka andaza laga kar jawab deta hai). In one sentence: it has read a huge amount of text, and it answers by predicting the words that most likely come next.

That is powerful, and it is also the catch. It predicts a likely answer, not always a true one. So you treat it as a smart helper, never as the final word.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

اے آئی ٹول ایک پروگرام ہے جس سے آپ عام زبان میں بات کرتے ہیں۔ یہ بہت سارا متن پڑھ چکا ہوتا ہے، اور جواب دیتے وقت یہ اندازہ لگاتا ہے کہ اگلا لفظ کیا ہو سکتا ہے۔ اسی لیے اس کا جواب ہمیشہ سچ نہیں ہوتا۔ اسے ایک مددگار سمجھیں، حتمی استاد نہیں۔

docs.title: The big AI tools people use today
docs.text:
AI moves fast. New models arrive every few months. Here is the short history, so you can see how quickly it grew.

A timeline of well-known AI chat models from 2020 to 2026: GPT-3 in 2020, ChatGPT in 2022, GPT-4 and Claude in 2023, Gemini in 2024, and the current GPT-5, Claude 4, and Gemini 3 families by 2026. GPT-3 2020 ChatGPT 2022 GPT-4, Claude 2023 Gemini 2024 GPT-5, Claude 4, Gemini 3 2026, now

From one big model in 2020 to a crowded, fast-moving field today. The names and version numbers keep changing, so always check for the newest one.

No single tool is best at everything, and the leader changes often. Here is an honest map of what to reach for, with free tiers you can start on today.

What you want to do Good options to try Ask questions, learn, explain code ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini Use a free or open model Llama, DeepSeek Make images Midjourney, DALL-E (inside ChatGPT), Gemini, Flux Help design and build interfaces v0, Figma, GitHub Copilot, Cursor

A few honest notes for a beginner in 2026:

  • For learning, writing, and code, the strongest models right now are the Claude family (this very book was built with Claude), the GPT-5 family inside ChatGPT, and Google's Gemini. Any of them is a fine study partner. Start with the free tier of one.
  • For images, Midjourney and the big chat tools all produce strong results. Open models like Flux and Stable Diffusion let you run things yourself.
  • For design and front-end code, tools like v0 and Copilot can generate interfaces. They are useful later. They are not for now, and the next section explains why.

docs.title: The one rule for now: ask, do not copy
docs.text:
This is the most important paragraph in the lesson. Read it twice.

While you are a beginner, do not let AI write your code

Use AI to ask questions and clear your confusions. Do not use it to write your code or do your exercises for you. If the machine writes it, the machine learned it, not you. You can lean on AI once the basics are truly yours, which is many months away. For now: ask, understand, then type it yourself.

Why so strict? Because writing code yourself is the whole point of the early months. Reading an AI answer feels like learning. It is not. The struggle of doing it by hand is where the skill forms. As the people who created the C language put it:

"The only way to learn a new programming language is by writing programs in it."

Brian Kernighan and Dennis Ritchie, The C Programming Language

Teachers and senior developers across the industry give beginners the same warning, and researchers studying AI and learning have found the same pattern: people who let AI do the thinking remember and understand far less of their own work. So let AI explain a topic, then close it and build the thing yourself.

Keep your hands on the wheel

A good test: if the AI writes something, ask it to walk you through each line until you could write that line again from memory, with the tool closed. If you cannot, you have not learned it yet.

docs.title: Ask a better question
docs.text:
A weak question gets a weak answer. A clear question gets a clear answer. Tell the AI three things: who you are, what you want, and the size or shape of the answer you need.

A weak <abbr title="The question or instruction you type into an AI tool. (Roman Urdu: woh sawaal ya hidayat jo tum AI ko likh kar dete ho)">prompt</abbr> that just says "explain html" next to a strong <abbr title="The question or instruction you type into an AI tool. (Roman Urdu: woh sawaal ya hidayat jo tum AI ko likh kar dete ho)">prompt</abbr> that gives your level, the topic, and the size of answer you want. WEAK "explain html" Too short. The AI guesses your level and dumps a wall of text. STRONG "I am a complete beginner. Explain what HTML is in simple English, with one small example, under 100 words." Your level, the topic, the size.

A weak prompt versus a strong one. The strong prompt gives your level, the topic, and the size of answer you want.

If the answer is still unclear, say so. "Explain that again like I am twelve" or "give me one more example" both work. The tool will happily try a different way.

docs.title: Check the answer, every time that matters
docs.text:
Sometimes an AI states something wrong as if it were true. This is called a hallucination (Roman Urdu: jab AI ghalat baat ko sach ki tarah keh de). It can sound very confident and still be incorrect.

So for anything important, check it:

  1. Ask the AI for a small example you can run yourself.
  2. Search the same thing on a trusted site like MDN.
  3. If the two agree, trust it. If they disagree, trust MDN.

Did you know

The word "hallucination" for AI mistakes became common only around 2023. The behaviour is older. Giving a problem a clear name is half of handling it.

docs.title: More ways to use AI every day
docs.text:
Once the strict rule is respected, AI is a genuine time-saver outside of writing your code. A few honest uses:

  • Summarise a long article or explain a hard paragraph in simpler words.
  • Explain an error message you do not understand, then fix it yourself.
  • Draft an email, a message to a client, or a study plan, then edit it in your own voice.
  • Translate between English and Urdu when a word is blocking you.
  • Brainstorm project ideas, or quiz you with practice questions before a test.

Never paste passwords or other people's private data into any AI tool. Treat it like a public place.

Later: an AI helper in your terminal

Further on, after you know JavaScript, you will install an AI coding assistant that lives right in your terminal, such as OpenCode or Claude Code. You do not need it now, and you should not. Note the name, and come back to it when the course tells you to, well after the JavaScript chapters.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open ChatGPT or Claude and write one strong prompt using the three parts from this lesson: who you are, what you want, and the size of the answer. For example: "I am a complete beginner.
