Explain what a web browser does in simple English, under 80 words, with one everyday example." Read the answer, then ask it to explain again "like I am twelve." Notice how a clear question gives a clear answer, and notice that you did not ask it to write any code.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. In one sentence, how does a large language model produce its answer?
  2. Name one AI tool good for learning, and one good for making images.
  3. What is the one strict rule for using AI while you are a beginner, and why?
  4. What three things make a question strong?
  5. What is a hallucination, and how do you guard against one?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You have a study partner and you know its limits. The last piece of getting ready is the skill under every other skill: how to learn well. The right order of video, reading, and practice, a calm routine for when you get stuck, and the focus habits that make hard things easier.

Next lesson: 0.4 Learning to learn →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • The Odin Project: Motivation and mindset why beginners should not lean on AI for code.
  • Anthropic: Prompt engineering overview how to ask clearly.
  • MDN: Learn web development the trusted site to check answers against.

docs.title: 0.4 Learning to learn
docs.text: Coding is not about being clever. It is about good habits and not giving up. The students who finish this course are rarely the smartest. They are the ones who show up, practise a little each day, and know what to do when they get stuck. This lesson hands you those habits.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• Why doing beats watching, every time
  • The right order to learn anything: watch, then read, then do
  • A calm routine for when you get stuck
  • How to focus in short blocks, and how to beat the urge to put things off

docs.title: Learn by doing, not just watching
docs.text:
Watching a tutorial and nodding along feels productive. It is not. Most of it is gone by the next day. This is passive learning.

Active learning is doing the thing yourself. You type the code, you break it, you fix it. It is slower and it feels harder, and that is exactly why it sticks.

So make this a rule: after every lesson, build the small example yourself, without copying. Get it wrong, look it up, try again. That struggle is the learning.

Tip: the one-minute test

After a lesson, close the page and rebuild the example from memory. You will forget bits. Good. Looking them up again is how they move into long-term memory.

docs.title: The right order: watch, then read, then do
docs.text:
When you meet something new, learn it in three steps, in this order. Most beginners get stuck because they only ever do the first step.

  1. Watch one short video to get the shape of the idea. One video, not ten. A video is great for a first overview, and a poor choice as your only teacher.
  2. Then read the docs, an article, or a book chapter on the same topic. Reading is slower, and it gives you the depth and the details a video skips. If you want to go deep in this field, you have to become a reader.
  3. Then practise it yourself. This is the real step. This is where the learning actually happens. The video and the reading were only preparation for this.

Watching ten videos and building nothing leaves you with nothing. One video, one article, and one thing you built yourself beats it every time.

docs.title: The 15-minute rule for getting unstuck
docs.text:
Everyone gets stuck. Being stuck is not failing. It is the normal sound of learning. Here is a routine that keeps you calm.

  1. Read the error. It often names the exact line and problem.
  2. Check the small things. A missing bracket or a wrong file name breaks a lot.
  3. Try for 15 minutes. Wrestle with it honestly. This is where you grow.
  4. Then ask, and ask well. Say what you expected, what happened, and show your code.

That last point matters. "It doesn't work" is hard to help. "I expected the button to turn green, but it stayed grey, here is my code" gets you an answer in seconds.

Did you know

Stepping away often solves the problem. Your brain keeps working in the background. Many bugs get fixed in the shower, not at the desk.

docs.title: Focus in short, honest blocks
docs.text:
Two small habits carry a lot of weight: short focused blocks, and a daily rhythm.

The most popular focus method in the world is the Pomodoro technique. It is simple: work with full focus for 25 minutes, then take a 5-minute break. After four rounds, take a longer break. The short timer makes a big task feel small, and the breaks keep your mind fresh.

A few mind tricks make starting easier, because starting is the hard part:

  • The two-minute rule. Tell yourself you will work for just two minutes. Once you start, you usually keep going. Starting is the whole battle.
  • Put the phone in another room. Not face down on the desk. In another room. You cannot resist a notification you never see.
  • One task at a time. Close the other tabs. Doing one thing well beats doing five things badly.
  • Little and often wins. Twenty focused minutes a day beats five hours once a week.

Keep a short notes file as you go. Three lines in your own words after each lesson is enough. Writing it down is a form of active recall, and active recall is how memory forms.

docs.title: Words to code by
docs.text:
Two lines worth remembering, from people who shaped this field:

"Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute."

Harold Abelson, Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs

"Practice isn't the thing you do once you're good. It's the thing you do that makes you good."

Malcolm Gladwell, Outliers

Write code that a human can read. And remember that practice is not what you do after you are good. Practice is what makes you good.

docs.title: A word on laziness
docs.text:
There is a short, powerful lesson in our tradition about putting things off.

The companion Ka'b ibn Malik missed the long march to Tabuk. Not because he doubted, and not because he could not go. He had two healthy camels and every means to join. He simply kept delaying. "I will get ready tomorrow," he told himself, day after day, until the army marched without him. He said later that he kept putting it off, again and again, until it was too late, and a long and bitter hardship followed. All of it grew from a habit that feels small: later.

Don't wait for 'later'

Laziness (Roman Urdu: susti) rarely looks like a big choice. It looks like one small "later" after another. The cure is the two-minute rule: do the lesson now, even a little. Effort today is worth far more than a perfect plan for tomorrow.

Retold in short from the long hadith of Ka'b ibn Malik, Sahih al-Bukhari 4418.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Go back to lesson 0.1, close it, and from memory write three lines in a notes file: what a file is, what a folder is, and what a browser does. Then open 0.1 and check yourself. The bits you got wrong are exactly the bits worth studying again. This is active recall in action, and it takes under five minutes.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. What is the difference between active and passive learning?
  2. What is the right order for learning something new, and which step is the real one?
  3. What are the first two steps when you get stuck?
  4. What is the Pomodoro technique, and what does the two-minute rule fix?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
That's Chapter 0 done. You can use your computer, search well, learn with AI, and study in a way that lasts. Now the real work starts. In the next chapter you set up your computer for proper frontend development and learn how the web fits together.

Start Chapter 1: Orientation and setup →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Coursera: Learning How to Learn the famous free course by Barbara Oakley on how memory works.
  • Book: Pragmatic Thinking and Learning by Andy Hunt, on learning as a developer.
  • The Odin Project: How this course will work a kind, honest take on the beginner journey.

docs.title: Chapter 1: Orientation and setup
docs.text:
Muhammad Umar Sajid · Trainer at Bano Qabil Sahiwal · Front End Development and Graphic Design

This is where the real work starts. First you see the kind of websites you will be able to build. Then you set up your computer like a working developer, learn to drive the terminal, and meet the browser tools you will use every single day.

Take the install lesson slowly. Once your tools are ready, the rest of the course flows.

docs.title: Lessons in this chapter
docs.text:
• 1.1 What is frontend development?: frontend, backend, and the kinds of sites you can build.
  • 1.2 Install everything: WSL2, Ubuntu, VS Code, Node, and Git, with a Windows-native fallback.
  • 1.3 The terminal: move around your computer by typing, and read errors calmly.
  • 1.4 Browser DevTools: inspect and edit any live web page in the browser.

No rush

Setting up tools can take an afternoon, especially on slow internet. That is normal. Finish what you can, take a break, and come back to it.

docs.title: Mega assignment
docs.text:
Choose your client and audit a real Pakistani website

For the rest of this course you will build a real one-page website for a real local business. Pick one now: a karyana store, a salon, a tutor, a tailor, a café, your uncle's shop, anyone you actually know who has no website or a weak one. By the end of this book, they will have something real.

What you build and do

  • A short client brief (half a page): the business name, what they sell, who their customers are, and three things their website must do (for example: show the menu, show location and hours, let people message on WhatsApp).
  • A DevTools audit of one live Pakistani site (Daraz, Foodpanda, or your client's competitor). In your journal, note three HTML elements you inspected, one message the Console shows, and one thing that is clearly frontend versus backend.
  • A verified toolchain: prove your setup actually works.

Done when

  • Your client brief is written and names a real business.
  • node -v, npm -v, and git --version each print a version in your terminal.
  • VS Code opens a folder and you can type in its integrated terminal.
  • Your DevTools audit notes are in your learning journal.

Stretch goal: Message the owner and ask what they wish their customers could do online. Real requirements always beat imagined ones.

Start lesson 1.1 →

docs.title: 1.1 What is frontend development?
docs.text: Open any website you like. The buttons, the menus, the photos, the colours: that is the front of the web. Someone wrote code to make all of it. By the end of this course, that someone is you. This lesson explains what that work is called and what you can build with it.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What frontend means, in plain words
  • How frontend differs from backend and full-stack
  • The kinds of websites you can build
  • Real Pakistani sites you already use as examples
  • A simple intention to start with

docs.title: Frontend, backend, full-stack
docs.text:
A website has two halves. There is the part you see and touch, and the part that works behind the scenes.

The frontend is the part users see and click, right inside the browser. The text, the layout, the buttons, the form you fill in: all frontend. You build it with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. This is what this whole course teaches.

The backend is the part users never see. It runs on a server far away. It saves your account, checks your password, and remembers what is in your cart. It sends the right data to the frontend.

A full-stack developer does both halves. They build the part you see and the part behind it. Many people start with frontend, then learn backend later.

Think of a restaurant. The frontend is the dining hall, the menu, and the waiter. The backend is the kitchen. The full-stack person can serve a table and cook the food.

Frontend is the part you see in the browser; <abbr title="The part of a website that runs on a server and handles data, accounts, and logic, which users never see. (Roman Urdu: website ka woh hissa jo server par chalta hai aur data sambhalta hai)">backend</abbr> runs on a server behind the scenes; a <abbr title="A developer who builds both the frontend and the backend of a website. (Roman Urdu: woh developer jo frontend aur backend dono banata hai)">full-stack</abbr> developer builds both. Frontend Backend asks for data sends data back what you see and click (the dining hall) runs on a server (the kitchen) A full-stack developer builds both halves

Frontend lives in the browser, backend lives on a server, and full-stack means building both. اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ویب سائٹ کے دو حصے ہوتے ہیں۔ فرنٹ اینڈ وہ حصہ ہے جو آپ براؤزر میں دیکھتے اور کلک کرتے ہیں، جیسے بٹن، مینو، اور فارم۔ بیک اینڈ وہ حصہ ہے جو پیچھے سرور پر چلتا ہے اور ڈیٹا سنبھالتا ہے۔ فل اسٹیک ڈیولپر دونوں حصے بناتا ہے۔ یہ کورس آپ کو فرنٹ اینڈ سکھاتا ہے۔

How your work reaches a person: you write code, the browser reads it, and a person sees a website.

You write code HTML, CSS, JS The browser reads it Chrome, Edge A person sees a website

Frontend work in one line: you write code, the browser reads it, and a person sees a website.

docs.title: What you can build
docs.text:
Frontend skills open the door to many kinds of sites. Here is a short list:

  • Portfolio: a page that shows your work to clients.
  • Ecommerce store: a shop where people browse and buy.
  • SaaS landing page: a page that sells an online tool.
  • Blog: a place to write and share articles.
  • Dashboard: a screen full of numbers and charts.
  • Charity or donation site: a page that collects help for a cause.
  • Restaurant menu: a clean page that lists food and prices.

You will build small versions of several of these as you go. The skills are the same across all of them.

docs.title: Sites you already use
docs.text:
You do not need foreign examples. You already use Pakistani sites built on these same skills. Look at how each one feels in the browser:

  • Daraz, an online shopping store.

  • Foodpanda, food delivery.

  • AlKhidmat, a charity and donation site.

  • Bykea, rides and delivery.

Every button you tap on these sites is frontend. Someone wrote that. Soon you will read their code and understand most of it.

docs.title: A note on what you build
docs.text:
You get to choose what you build. You can make useful, honest sites that help people and respect them. You can skip haram industries, gambling, and interest. You can also skip designs that trick users, like hidden charges or fake timers that rush a sale. A site can be both beautiful and fair. That choice is yours from the first line of code.

There is a well-loved teaching story about niyyah (نیّت), the intention behind what you do. A companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, built a house with a window. When the Prophet, peace be upon him, asked why he had made the window, the man said it was to let in sunlight and fresh air. The Prophet, peace be upon him, told him that if he also intended to hear the adhan, the call to prayer, through that window, he would be rewarded for that intention too, while still enjoying the light and the air. The window did not change. Only the niyyah did.

Your code is that window. The same work, built with a good intention, becomes more than just work.

A note on niyyah (نیّت)

Before you learn a skill, it helps to set your intention. You can learn this to earn a halal income and support your family. You can learn it to be useful to people who need a good website. You can learn it to make beautiful things with care. A clear niyyah makes the long hours feel lighter.

This window story is a well-known traditional anecdote used to teach niyyah, passed on as an oral lesson rather than a single verified narration.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open Daraz or Foodpanda in your browser. Pick one page and write down five things on it that are frontend: for example a button, the menu, a product photo, a search box, the colours. Then write one line guessing what the backend does when you tap the search box. This trains your eye to see the two halves on every site you use.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. In one sentence, what is the frontend?
  2. What does the backend do that the frontend does not?
  3. What is a full-stack developer?
  4. Name three kinds of sites you could build with frontend skills.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You know what frontend is and what you can build with it. Next you set up the tools. You will install a browser, a code editor, and a few helpers, so your laptop is ready to write real code.

Next lesson: 1.2 Install everything →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • MDN: Front-end web developer and web standards a clear overview of what the work involves.
  • The Odin Project: Foundations a free, well-loved path through the basics.

docs.title: 1.2 Install everything
docs.text:
This is the big setup lesson. By the end your computer, whether it runs Windows, macOS, or Linux, will be a real coding machine: a proper terminal, a code editor, Node.js, Git, and a GitHub account. Take your time and do the steps in order.

This is the longest lesson in the course

Plan for about an hour, and do not feel you must finish it in one sitting. After each step there is a checkpoint. Stop at any checkpoint, take a break, and come back later. The checkpoints tell you exactly where you are.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What an operating system is, and which one suits coding
  • How to get a proper terminal on your system
  • How to install Node.js, VS Code, and Git
  • Why we use an IDE, and why VS Code first
  • How to create a GitHub account and connect Git to it

docs.title: A quick word on operating systems
docs.text:
An operating system (Roman Urdu: woh bunyadi software jo poora computer chalata hai), or OS, is the software that runs your whole computer. It sits between you and the machine. Your apps talk to the OS, and the OS talks to the hardware. Every program you open, from Chrome to a game, runs on top of it.

Three stacked layers: at the top are you and your apps, in the middle is the <abbr title="The software that runs your whole computer and sits between your apps and the hardware. Windows, macOS, and Linux are operating systems. (Roman Urdu: woh bunyadi software jo poora computer chalata hai)">operating system</abbr> that manages everything, and at the bottom is the hardware. You and your apps Chrome, VS Code, WhatsApp, games Operating system Windows, macOS, or Linux: the manager in the middle Hardware the chip, memory, screen, and storage via the OS

The operating system is the manager in the middle. Your apps never touch the hardware directly; they ask the OS, and the OS handles it.

docs.title: The big three
docs.text:
Almost every computer runs one of three operating systems. Each has a logo you already know.

  • Windows, by Microsoft. The most common OS on laptops and in homes, schools, and offices.
  • macOS, by Apple. Runs only on Apple computers (MacBook, iMac).
  • Linux, free and open. It powers most servers on the internet, and many developers use it on their own machines too.

docs.title: What changes when you switch OS
docs.text:
Moving to a different OS is like moving to a city with different rules. The big differences you feel are:

  • The apps. Some programs are made for one OS only. A .exe installer is for Windows; a .dmg is for macOS.
  • The file paths. Windows writes C:\Users\Ali, while macOS and Linux write /home/ali.
  • The terminal. Windows uses PowerShell; macOS and Linux use a Unix shell. Most coding tutorials online assume the Unix shell.
  • Installing software. Linux and macOS install most tools with one command in the terminal. Windows usually uses installers you download and click.

docs.title: Which OS is best for coding?
docs.text:
The honest answer: all three work. But they are not equal for a beginner who wants to code. Here is how they compare.

Windows macOS Linux Easy for a beginner Very easy Easy Takes some learning Ready for coding tools Good, great with WSL2 Very good Excellent Comes with the machine Most laptops Apple devices only You install it free Cost Included with most laptops Apple hardware, costly Free for any computer Security Good, but a big target, so keep it updated Very good Very good Who uses it Most people and students Many developers and designers Servers and many developers

A rough bar chart of the main <abbr title="The software that runs your whole computer and sits between your apps and the hardware. Windows, macOS, and Linux are operating systems. (Roman Urdu: woh bunyadi software jo poora computer chalata hai)">operating system</abbr> on professional developers' computers: Windows around 47 percent, macOS around 33 percent, Linux around 20 percent. Windows macOS Linux about 47% about 33% about 20%

Roughly how professional developers' main computers split. It is approximate and shifts every year, but it shows that all three are common.

What we do in this course

If you use Windows, you do not have to choose between Windows and Linux. You will run Linux inside Windows with a tool called WSL2. You keep all your normal Windows apps, and you also get the Linux terminal that coding tools love.

A big Windows box containing your normal apps on the left, and a smaller <abbr title="A popular kind of Linux. It is what WSL2 installs by default. (Roman Urdu: Linux ki ek mashhoor qism)">Ubuntu</abbr> Linux box on the right that holds your coding tools. <abbr title="Windows Subsystem for Linux, version 2. It runs a real Linux system inside Windows. (Roman Urdu: Windows ke andar Linux chalane ka tareeqa)">WSL2</abbr> runs the Linux box inside Windows. Windows Your normal apps: Chrome, Word, games Ubuntu (Linux) via WSL2 your terminal, Node.js, Git, the coding tools

WSL2 puts a real Linux inside Windows. You get Windows apps and Linux coding tools on one machine.

These are big downloads

The tools below are large, and on Windows WSL2 also downloads Ubuntu. Use good internet if you can, and plug in your charger, since some steps restart the computer.

docs.title: Step 1: Get a working terminal
docs.text:
This first part is the one that differs by operating system, so pick your tab.

Windows 11Windows 10 macOS Linux

Windows 11 can run Linux inside it with WSL2, giving you the tools developers expect without leaving Windows.

  1. Click Start and type PowerShell.
  2. Right click Windows PowerShell, choose Run as administrator, and click Yes.

  3. Type this command and press Enter:

    wsl --install

  4. Wait for it to finish. It downloads Ubuntu Linux, so this takes a few minutes.

  5. When it asks you to reboot, save your work and restart.

Windows 10 works the same way, with one extra step on older builds.

  1. Click Start, type PowerShell, right click it and choose Run as administrator.
  2. Run wsl --install. On recent Windows 10 this is all you need.
  3. If that command fails, turn on two features first: click Start, type Windows Features, open Turn Windows features on or off, tick Virtual Machine Platform and Windows Subsystem for Linux, click OK, and reboot. Then run wsl --install again.

macOS already ships with a Unix terminal, so there is no WSL to install.

  1. Press Cmd + Space, type Terminal, and press Enter to open it.
  2. Install Homebrew, the macOS package manager, by following the one command on brew.sh. You will use it to install the other tools.

You are already on Linux, so you have a terminal and a package manager.

  1. Open your terminal, often with Ctrl + Alt + T.
  2. That is it. Where Windows users run Ubuntu inside WSL, you use your system directly.

Windows: if your laptop can't run WSL2

Some older laptops cannot run WSL2, and that is fine. You can do the whole course the Windows native way instead. Skip the Ubuntu step below, and install the normal Windows versions of Node.js, VS Code, and Git from the download links in each step. When a lesson shows a bash command, run it in the Git Bash terminal that comes with Git for Windows.

docs.title: Step 2: Finish your Linux setup (Windows users)
docs.text:
macOS and Linux users

You already have your terminal, so you can skip this step and go to Step 3.

After the restart, Ubuntu opens on its own to finish setup. If it does not, click Start and click Ubuntu.

  1. Wait while Ubuntu installs. The first run takes a minute.
  2. When asked, type a new UNIX username. Use lowercase letters, like ali.
  3. When asked, type a password and press Enter, then type it again to confirm.

The password does not show on screen as you type. You will see nothing move, no dots, no stars. That is normal, and is how Linux hides it. Type carefully and press Enter.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

جب آپ Ubuntu میں پاس ورڈ ٹائپ کرتے ہیں تو اسکرین پر کچھ نظر نہیں آتا، نہ حروف نہ نقطے۔ یہ خرابی نہیں ہے۔ لینکس میں پاس ورڈ اسی طرح چھپا رہتا ہے تاکہ کوئی دیکھ نہ سکے۔ آرام سے ٹائپ کریں اور Enter دبائیں۔

Remember this password. You will need it when you install things later.

How to close Ubuntu and open it again

You can close the Ubuntu window any time to exit. To start it again later, you have two easy ways: click Start and type Ubuntu, or open any terminal and type wsl. Both drop you straight back into your Linux machine.

Checkpoint 1: your terminal is ready

Windows users have Ubuntu set up; macOS and Linux users have their terminal. A safe place to stop. Next you install your code editor.

docs.title: Step 3: Install VS Code (your code editor)
What is an IDE, and why VS Code?
docs.text:
An IDE (Roman Urdu: aik hi app jisme code likhne ke saare auzaar hote hain), short for integrated development environment, is one app that brings together everything you need to write code: a text editor, a file explorer, a built-in terminal, search, and add-ons. A plain text editor only edits text. An IDE helps you write, run, and fix code all in one place.

Why VS Code? It is free, fast, runs on every OS, and is by far the most popular editor among developers, so help and add-ons are everywhere. There are others: WebStorm is powerful but paid, Sublime Text and Zed are very fast and light, and Cursor is VS Code with AI built in. You can try them later. We all use VS Code here so we are looking at the same screen.

A few VS Code shortcuts worth learning now

Learn them one at a time. They save you hours.

  • Ctrl + S save the file
  • Ctrl + / comment or uncomment the current line
  • Ctrl + ` open or close the built-in terminal
  • Alt + Up and Alt + Down move the current line up or down
  • Ctrl + P jump to any file by typing its name
  • Ctrl + Shift + P open the command palette, which runs any VS Code command

VS Code also has a time-saver called Emmet that turns short abbreviations into full HTML. You will meet it in the HTML chapter.

docs.title: Install it
docs.text:
Download VS Code from code.visualstudio.com/download and pick your system, then follow your tab.

Windows macOS Linux
  1. Run the installer you downloaded and accept the defaults.
  2. Open VS Code, click the Extensions icon on the left bar (it looks like four small squares).
  3. Search for WSL and install the WSL extension by Microsoft.
  4. Open your Ubuntu terminal and type code . to open VS Code connected to Linux.
  5. Look at the bottom-left corner for a small green box. That green box means VS Code is running inside Ubuntu, which is what you want.
  1. Open the downloaded file and drag Visual Studio Code into your Applications folder.
  2. Open it from Applications, or with Cmd + Space then type Code.
  1. Install the .deb or .rpm you downloaded, or run sudo snap install code --classic.
  2. Open it from your apps menu, or type code . in a terminal.

Checkpoint 2: your editor is ready

VS Code is installed (and on Windows it connects to Ubuntu with the green box). Next you install Node.js.

docs.title: Step 4: Install Node.js
docs.text:
Node.js lets you run JavaScript outside the browser, and it comes with npm, the tool that installs code packages. You will use both constantly.

Open nodejs.org/en/download. This page builds the exact install commands for your system. Make sure npm is selected, it almost always is. If you see a Docker option and plan to use it, pick that too. The site is smart and usually detects the right choices for you. This is just in case.

Windows (in Ubuntu), Linux, macOSWindows without WSL

Use nvm, which installs Node.js and lets you switch versions later without breaking anything. In your terminal:

  1. Install nvm:

    curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.1/install.sh | bash

  2. Close the terminal and open it again, so nvm loads.

  3. Install the latest long-term-support version of Node:

    nvm install --lts

  4. Check that both work. You should see two version numbers:

    node -v
npm -v
  1. On the download page, choose the Windows Installer (.msi) for the LTS version.
  2. Run it and accept the defaults.
  3. Open a new terminal and check it worked: node -v and npm -v should each print a version number.

Checkpoint 3: Node.js works

node -v and npm -v both print version numbers. npm comes with Node, so you do not install it separately.

docs.title: Step 5: Create your GitHub account first
docs.text:
GitHub is a website where you store your code online and share it. We make the account now, before Git, because Git needs to match it.

  1. Open github.com and click Sign up.
  2. Use an email you check often, for example your Gmail.
  3. Pick a username you are happy to show people. It becomes part of your web address.
  4. Confirm your email when GitHub sends you a message.

Remember these two things

Write down the email and the username you chose. In the next step you will give Git the exact same email and username, so your commits link to this GitHub account.

docs.title: Step 6: Install and set up Git
docs.text:
Git tracks the history of your code. Install it, then connect it to the GitHub account you just made.

Windows (in Ubuntu), LinuxWindows without WSL macOS

Install Git from your terminal:

sudo apt update && sudo apt install git

Type your Ubuntu password if it asks, then press y to confirm.

Download Git for Windows from git-scm.com/install/windows and run the installer. It also gives you the Git Bash terminal.

Run brew install git, or just type git --version and macOS will offer to install the developer tools that include Git.

Now tell Git who you are. Use the same username and email as your GitHub account:

# use the same username you chose on GitHub
git config --global user.name "your-github-username"
# use the same email you signed up to GitHub with
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

Checkpoint 4: you are fully set up

Git is installed and linked to your GitHub account, with the matching username and email. Your computer is now a real coding machine.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. In one sentence, what does an operating system do?
  2. On Windows, what does WSL2 give you that plain Windows does not?
  3. Why does the password show nothing while you type it in Ubuntu?
  4. Why do we create the GitHub account before setting up Git?
  5. Which two commands check that Node.js and npm work?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Your computer is now a real coding machine. You have a terminal, VS Code, Node.js, Git, and a GitHub account. Next you will learn the terminal properly, the text window where you typed those commands. It is faster than the mouse once you know it.

Next lesson: 1.3 The terminal →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Microsoft: Install WSL the full official guide.
  • VS Code: Developing in WSL how the WSL extension works.
  • nvm: nvm on GitHub the official project page.

docs.title: 1.3 The terminal
docs.text: You have clicked folders open with a mouse your whole life. The terminal does the same jobs by typing. It feels slow at first. Then it becomes the fastest way to move around your computer. Developers live in it.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What a terminal is, and why developers use it
  • The different terminal apps on Windows and macOS, and which to use
  • How to read the prompt, the text already sitting there
  • The everyday commands for moving and making files
  • How to make it your own, with colours and a few fun commands

docs.title: What a terminal is
docs.text:
A terminal is a window where you type commands instead of clicking. You type one line, press Enter, and the computer does it. The program that reads your line and runs it is called a shell (Roman Urdu: woh program jo terminal mein tumhari command parh kar chalata hai).

Why bother, when clicking works? Three reasons. It is faster once you know it. It works the same on every machine. And almost every developer tool, like Git and Node, expects you to use it.

A <abbr title="A window where you type commands instead of clicking. (Roman Urdu: woh window jahan tum mouse ke bajaye command type karte ho)">terminal</abbr> window mockup. It labels the <abbr title="The text already in the terminal, ending in $, that shows who and where you are and waits for your command. (Roman Urdu: terminal mein pehle se mojood likhai jo tumhari command ka intezar karti hai)">prompt</abbr>, the current folder shown in the <abbr title="The text already in the terminal, ending in $, that shows who and where you are and waits for your command. (Roman Urdu: terminal mein pehle se mojood likhai jo tumhari command ka intezar karti hai)">prompt</abbr>, the <abbr title="One typed instruction you give the shell, like ls or cd. (Roman Urdu: ek likhi hui hidayat jo tum shell ko dete ho)">command</abbr> you typed, and the output printed below. Ubuntu (bash) ali@laptop:~/projects$ ls notes.txt site images ali@laptop:~/projects$ the prompt current folder command you typed the output below

A terminal window. The prompt waits for you. It shows who you are and the current folder. You type a command, press Enter, and the output prints below.

docs.title: Reading the prompt
docs.text:
When you open the terminal, there is already some text sitting there, ending in a $. That is the prompt, and it is not random. It tells you who and where you are. A typical Linux prompt looks like this:

ali@laptop:~/projects$

Here is what each part means:

Part What it means ali your username on the computer @laptop the name of the computer you are on ~ your home folder, your starting place ~/projects the projects folder inside your home folder $ the prompt symbol; you type your command right after it

So ali@laptop:~/projects$ simply says: "Ali, on the laptop, currently inside the projects folder, ready for a command." After you type and press Enter, the answer prints below, and a fresh prompt appears.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ٹرمینل کھولتے ہی جو لکھائی پہلے سے موجود ہوتی ہے، اسے پرامپٹ کہتے ہیں۔ اس میں آپ کا یوزر نیم، کمپیوٹر کا نام، اور وہ فولڈر دکھائی دیتا ہے جس میں آپ اس وقت ہیں۔ ~ کا مطلب آپ کا ہوم فولڈر ہے۔ $ کے بعد آپ اپنی کمانڈ لکھتے ہیں اور Enter دباتے ہیں۔

docs.title: Which terminal app should I open?
docs.text:
Different systems give you different terminal apps. Pick your tab.

Windows macOS Linux

Windows actually has a few, which confuses beginners:

  • Command Prompt (cmd) is the old, basic one. It still works, but it is limited.
  • PowerShell is Microsoft's newer, more powerful shell.
  • Windows Terminal is a modern app that holds all of them in tabs: PowerShell, Command Prompt, and your Ubuntu (WSL) shell. If you do not have it, install Windows Terminal free from the Microsoft Store.
  • For this course you use the Ubuntu (bash) shell from WSL. Open it by typing wsl in any terminal, or by opening Ubuntu from Start.

macOS comes with the built-in Terminal app. Open it with Cmd + Space, then type Terminal. It runs a Unix shell called zsh, which is very close to bash, so every command in this course works. (iTerm2 is a popular fancier option, but it is optional.)

Your system has a terminal app already, usually opened with Ctrl + Alt + T. It runs bash or zsh. The commands in this course are made for it.

We use bash in this course for one simple reason: almost every tutorial, error guide, and teammate online speaks bash. When you copy a command from the internet, it usually expects bash.

docs.title: What can you do with the terminal?
docs.text:
A lot. Here are the everyday jobs you will use it for:

  • Move around your files and folders, faster than clicking.
  • Create, copy, move, and delete files.
  • Install tools, the way you installed Node.js and Git in the last lesson.
  • Run your projects, with commands like npm run dev.
  • Use Git to save and share your code.
  • Run small helpful programs, and a few fun ones too.

docs.title: The everyday commands
docs.text:
Here are the commands you will use every day. Try each one as you read. Type it, press Enter, and watch what happens.

pwd prints the folder you are in right now. The letters mean "print working directory".

# show the full path of where you are
pwd

ls lists what is inside the current folder.

# list files and folders here
ls

cd changes the folder you are in. The letters mean "change directory".

# move into a folder called projects
cd projects
# go back up one level to the parent folder
cd ..

mkdir makes a new folder.

# make a folder called site
mkdir site

touch makes a new empty file.

# make an empty file called index.html
touch index.html

rm removes a file.

# delete a file called old.txt
rm old.txt

rm does not use the Recycle Bin

When you delete a file with rm, it is gone. There is no Recycle Bin to get it back. Read the file name twice before you press Enter.

cp copies a file. You give it the source first, then the new name.

# copy notes.txt to a new file called backup.txt
cp notes.txt backup.txt

mv moves or renames a file. Same shape: old name first, new name second.

# rename draft.txt to final.txt
mv draft.txt final.txt

docs.title: Send output with a pipe and a redirect
docs.text:
Two small symbols make the terminal feel powerful. You will meet them often.

A pipe (Roman Urdu: woh | nishan jo ek command ka natija dusri command ko deta hai) is the | symbol. It takes the output of one command and feeds it into the next. Think of it as a tube between two commands.

# list files, then count how many lines that list has
ls | wc -l

The > symbol redirects output into a file instead of the screen. If the file exists, it gets overwritten.

# write the text into a new file called hello.txt
echo "Salaam dunya" > hello.txt

After that second command, open hello.txt and you will see your text inside. The words never printed on screen. They went straight into the file.

docs.title: Read an error message calmly
docs.text:
Errors look scary. They are not. An error message is the computer telling you exactly what stopped it. The trick is simple: read the last line first.

# try to enter a folder that does not exist
cd projcts

bash: cd: projcts: No such file or directory

Read the last part: "No such file or directory". The folder name is wrong. You typed projcts instead of projects. Fix the spelling and try again. Most errors are this small.

docs.title: Make it yours: colours and fun
docs.text: The terminal does not have to be a plain black box. Making it pretty makes you want to spend time in it, and spending time in it is how you get fast.

docs.title: Change the colours
docs.text:
Windows macOS Linux

In Windows Terminal, press Ctrl + , to open Settings, choose your profile, and pick a Color scheme like "One Half Dark" or "Campbell". In VS Code's built-in terminal, the colours follow your editor theme.

Open Terminal, then Terminal > Settings > Profiles, and pick a theme like "Pro" or "Homebrew". Click the default button to keep it.

Open your terminal app's Preferences or Profile settings and choose a colour scheme. Most terminals ship with several.

docs.title: Run a few fun commands
docs.text:
These little programs are pure fun, and they prove your terminal works. On Ubuntu or Linux, install three of them at once:

# install three playful tools
sudo apt install cowsay figlet lolcat

Now try them:

# a cow says whatever you type
cowsay "Salaam, BanoQabil!"
# turn text into big ASCII letters
figlet "Hello"
# add rainbow colours by piping into lolcat
figlet "Hello" | lolcat

cowsay draws a cow with a speech bubble, figlet makes giant letters out of text, and lolcat paints anything you pipe into it in rainbow colours. Notice the | pipe from the last section doing real work.

Show off your machine

Try neofetch (install with sudo apt install neofetch). It prints your system details next to a piece of logo art. If your system does not have it, its modern replacement is fastfetch. On macOS, install these with brew install cowsay figlet instead of apt.

docs.title: Try this (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Open your terminal and follow along. Type each line, press Enter, and watch the result.

# make a folder to practice in
mkdir practice
# move into it
cd practice
# create an empty file
touch hello.txt
# list the folder to see your file
ls
# remove the file (remember: no Recycle Bin)
rm hello.txt

If ls showed hello.txt, you did it right. You just made a folder, entered it, created a file, listed it, and removed it. That is the daily work of the terminal. For a reward, run cowsay "I can use the terminal" and enjoy.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up.

  1. In the prompt ali@laptop:~/projects$, what does the ~ mean?
  2. On Windows, what is the difference between Command Prompt and Windows Terminal?
  3. What does the pwd command tell you?
  4. Why is there no Recycle Bin when you use rm?
  5. What does the | symbol do between two commands?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You can move around your computer by typing now. Next you will open the browser's built-in tools, where you inspect a live web page and watch it change.

Next lesson: 1.4 Browser DevTools →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Ubuntu: The Linux command line for beginners a gentle tour of bash.
  • The Odin Project: Command line basics more practice with the same commands.
  • MDN: Command line crash course the developer view of the terminal.

docs.title: 1.4 Browser DevTools
docs.text: Every website you visit is just code your browser drew on the screen. Hidden inside your browser is a set of tools that lets you open that code and look inside. These are the developer tools, or DevTools. Every frontend developer lives in them, all day, every day.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What DevTools are and why you will use them constantly
  • How to open DevTools in Chrome on Windows
  • How to read and edit a page's HTML and CSS in the Elements panel
  • How to see errors and run JavaScript in the Console
  • How to watch files load in the Network panel
  • How to preview a page as a phone
  • Why your DevTools edits are only visible to you

docs.title: What DevTools are
docs.text:
DevTools is a panel built into your browser. It shows you the code behind any page. You can read it, change it, and test things, all in real time.

You did not install anything for this. Chrome, Edge, and Firefox all ship with DevTools already inside. We use Chrome on Windows here, since that is what most of you have.

Why does this matter so much? When your page looks wrong, DevTools shows you why. When something breaks, the Console tells you what broke. You stop guessing. You start seeing.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ڈیو ٹولز آپ کے براؤزر کے اندر چھپا ہوا ایک پینل ہے۔ یہ کسی بھی صفحے کا کوڈ آپ کو دکھاتا ہے۔ آپ اسے پڑھ سکتے ہیں، بدل سکتے ہیں، اور آزما سکتے ہیں۔ جب صفحہ ٹھیک نہ دکھے، تو ڈیو ٹولز بتاتا ہے کہ مسئلہ کہاں ہے۔

docs.title: Which browser should you use?
docs.text:
You can open a website in many browsers, and most of them are built on the same engine under the hood.

  • Chromium-based: Chrome, Edge, Brave, and Opera all share Google's Chromium engine, so they look and inspect almost the same.
  • Firefox uses its own engine, Gecko, and its own DevTools.
  • Safari uses Apple's engine, WebKit, and runs only on Apple devices.

Approximate global browser share: Chrome about 65 percent, Safari about 18 percent, Edge about 5 percent, Firefox about 3 percent, and others about 9 percent. Chrome Safari Edge Firefox Others about 65% about 18% about 5% about 3% about 9%

Roughly how the world's browsers split today. It is approximate and changes over time, but Chrome's lead is a big reason most tutorials use it. Browser Built on DevTools Good to know Chrome Chromium Excellent What we use. Most tutorials and extensions. Edge Chromium Excellent Almost identical to Chrome. Brave Chromium Excellent Chrome-like, privacy focused. Firefox Gecko Very good Great, but its DevTools sit in different places. Safari WebKit Good Apple devices only. Test it if your users are on iPhone.

We use Chrome in this course because it is the most popular, its DevTools are the most complete, and almost every tutorial you find online shows Chrome. If you prefer a different browser, here is what to expect:

  • Edge or Brave: you are basically using Chrome under the hood, so every step in this course works the same.
  • Firefox: everything works, but the menus and DevTools panels sit in different places, so our screenshots will not match exactly.
  • Safari: it runs only on Mac and iPhone, its DevTools look quite different and must be turned on first in Settings. Fine as a second browser, not the easiest to follow this course with.

docs.title: Open DevTools
docs.text:
There are two easy ways to open DevTools in Chrome:

  1. Press F12 on your keyboard.
  2. Or right-click anywhere on a page and choose Inspect.

Both do the same thing. A panel opens at the side or bottom of your window. The right-click way is handy because it jumps straight to the thing you clicked.

Tip: dock it where you like

Click the three dots inside DevTools to move the panel. You can dock it bottom, left, or right, or pop it into its own window.

To close DevTools, press F12 again.

docs.title: The Elements panel
docs.text:
The Elements panel shows the page's HTML as a DOM tree (Roman Urdu: page ke HTML ka darakht jaisa khaaka). Each line is one piece of the page: a heading, a paragraph, an image, a button. Click a line and that part lights up on the page. Click the small triangles to open and close the nested pieces.

There are two quick ways to land on the exact element you want:

  • Right-click anything on the page and choose Inspect. DevTools jumps straight to that element in the tree.
  • Or click the inspect arrow icon at the top-left of DevTools, then move your mouse over the page. Each element highlights as you hover.

On the side you see the Styles pane: the CSS for the selected element. CSS is the code that sets colours, sizes, and spacing. Change a value there and the page updates instantly.

docs.title: Handy moves in the Elements panel
docs.text: To do this Do this Edit the text of an element double-click the text and type Edit the raw HTML of an element right-click the line and choose Edit as HTML, or press F2 Change a colour or size edit a value in the Styles pane Hide an element click it and press H Delete an element click it and press Delete

docs.title: Low level hacking (a joke, but a fun one)
docs.text:
Let us "hack" a famous website. We are not really hacking anything, but it does feel like it.

  1. Open google.com in Chrome.
  2. Right-click the Google Search button and choose Inspect.
  3. The button's line lights up in the Elements panel. Press F2, or right-click the line and choose Edit as HTML.
  4. Change the button's text to your name, like Ali Search, and press Enter.
  5. Look at the page. The button now reads Ali Search. You just "edited" Google.

Show a friend and watch their face. Then read the next part, because there is an honest catch.

docs.title: Why only you can see it
docs.text:
You did not change Google for the world. You changed it only on your own screen, for this one moment. Here is why.

The server holds the real website files and sends your browser a copy. Your edits in <abbr title="A set of tools built into the browser for inspecting and editing web pages. (Roman Urdu: browser ke andar bana tool jo page ka code dikhata aur badalta hai)">DevTools</abbr> change only that copy. A refresh asks the server again and gets the original back. The server holds the real website files Your browser a copy drawn on your screen 1. sends a copy 3. refresh gets the original 2. your edits change only this copy

The server sends your browser a copy of the page. Your DevTools edits change only that copy. A refresh fetches the original again, so your changes vanish.

When you open a website, the server sends your browser a copy of the files, and your browser draws that copy on your screen. When you edit in DevTools, you are changing only that copy, the one sitting in your browser's memory. The real files on the server never change, and nobody else's browser ever saw your edit.

That is also why a refresh undoes everything. Refreshing tells your browser to throw away its copy and ask the server for a fresh one. The server sends the original, so the page snaps right back to normal.

You cannot break the internet

Editing in DevTools is completely safe to explore. Nobody else sees your changes, and the real site is untouched. Poke at anything.

docs.title: The Console panel
docs.text:
The Console is where the browser talks to you. When a page has an error, the Console shows it in red. Learning to read these messages is a core skill.

The Console also runs JavaScript. You type a line, press Enter, and it runs at once. This is the fastest way to test a small idea.

Click the Console tab, click next to the > symbol, and type this:

console.log("Salaam");

Press Enter. The word Salaam prints back to you. You just ran your first line of JavaScript. The console.log command prints whatever you put in the brackets.

Did you know

console.log is the most used line in all of JavaScript. Even developers with twenty years of work still drop it in to check what a value is.

docs.title: The Network panel
docs.text:
When a page opens, it pulls in many files: the HTML, the CSS, images, fonts, and more. The Network panel shows each one loading, in order, with its size and its time.

Open the Network tab, then refresh the page. A list fills up as files arrive. A slow or heavy file sticks out, since its bar is long.

Screenshot coming soon

Chrome on Windows 11 with the Network panel open during a page load, showing the waterfall list of files with names, sizes, and timing bars.

This panel matters a lot in Pakistan. Many users wait on slow connections. The Network panel shows you which file is making them wait, so you can fix it.

docs.title: The Device toolbar
docs.text:
Most people in Pakistan browse on phones, not laptops. So your page must look good on a small screen first. The Device toolbar lets you preview that without owning ten phones.

Inside DevTools, click the small phone-and-tablet icon near the top left. The page shrinks to a phone-sized view. A menu at the top lets you pick a model, like an iPhone or a common Android size.

The Device toolbar previews a phone shape right inside your laptop, which is enough for most checks. When you want to see your site inside many different phone and tablet frames quickly, you can also install a Chrome extension like Mobile Simulator. It opens your page in a range of real device sizes side by side, which is handy before you hand a site to a client.

Tip: design for the phone first

Check your phone view often as you build. It is much easier than fixing a broken mobile layout at the end.

docs.title: Try this (5 minutes)
docs.text:
1. Open Chrome and go to developer.mozilla.org.
  2. Press F12 to open DevTools.
  3. Click the Elements tab. Find the main heading in the tree.
  4. Double-click its text and type your own name. Watch the page change.
  5. Click the Console tab. Type console.log("Salaam") and press Enter.
  6. Now refresh the page with F5. Your name is gone, since edits are local.

You just inspected a live website, edited it, and ran code. This is the daily work of a frontend developer.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. What are two ways to open DevTools in Chrome?
  2. What does the Elements panel let you see and change?
  3. What happens to your DevTools edits when you refresh the page?
  4. What does console.log("Salaam") do?
  5. Why does the Device toolbar matter for a Pakistani audience?

You finished Chapter 1

You set up your tools and learned to look inside any web page. That is a real start, and the hard part of getting ready is now behind you.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text:
Build your toolkit, and the habit of finding tools yourself

A developer's real skill is not memorising tools, it is knowing how to search for the right one and figure it out. Practise that habit now, because you will use it every day for the rest of your career.

What you build

  • A short "my toolkit" list in your learning journal: each tool you tried, what it does, and whether you will keep it.
  • VS Code extensions: open the Extensions panel and also search the web for what beginners use. Try a few (for example a live preview, a code formatter, a bracket or icon helper) and note which ones actually help you.
  • Chrome extensions: find one or two that help front-end work, like the Mobile Simulator from this lesson, a colour picker, or a font identifier.
  • Other tools: search something like "best free tools for web developers", skim a few lists, and pick two that look useful. Write down why.

Done when

  • You installed and tried at least three VS Code extensions and noted what each does.
  • You found at least one Chrome extension that helps your work.
  • Your journal has a short toolkit list written in your own words.

Stretch goal: For one tool you liked, open its documentation page and write down one thing you learned that the install page did not tell you.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Chapter 2 begins the real building. You will write your first HTML, the code that gives every web page its structure and content. Everything you inspected today, you will now create yourself.

Start Chapter 2: HTML →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Chrome: DevTools Elements panel docs the official guide this lesson's Elements section draws on.
  • Chrome: Chrome DevTools docs the full official guide to every panel.
  • MDN: What are browser developer tools? a friendly tour of the basics.

The Elements panel section above is based on Google's official Chrome DevTools documentation. With thanks and credit to the Chrome DevTools team.

docs.title: Chapter 2: HTML
docs.text:
Muhammad Umar Sajid · Trainer at Bano Qabil Sahiwal · Front End Development and Graphic Design

HTML is the skeleton of every web page. It holds the headings, the paragraphs, the images, the links, and the forms. Before a page can look good or do anything clever, it needs this structure underneath.

This chapter starts with the big picture of the three web languages, then has you write your first real HTML page in VS Code. From there you add text, lists, links, and images, build a contact form that looks like the real thing, and finally learn to give your pages meaning so search engines and screen readers understand them.

docs.title: Lessons in this chapter
docs.text:
• 2.1 HTML, CSS, and JavaScript: the three languages of the web, what each does, and where frameworks fit in.
  • 2.2 Your first HTML page: set up VS Code, write the page skeleton by hand, then with the emmet shortcut.
  • 2.3 Text, lists, links, and images: mark up real content, link pages together, and place images.
  • 2.4 Forms and inputs: build a contact form with every common input, label, and button.
  • 2.5 Semantic HTML and accessibility: name the parts of your page so everyone, and Google, can use it.

Type the code yourself

Reading HTML is easy. Writing it is the skill. Type every example into your own file and open it in the browser. You learn far more from your own typos than from clean code on a screen.

docs.title: Mega assignment
docs.text:
Hand-code your client's page structure, no styling yet

Structure first. Build the full skeleton of your client's one-page site in pure, semantic HTML. It will look plain, and that is exactly right. A strong skeleton is what makes every later chapter easy.

What you build

  • One index.html with: a header and nav, a hero with the business name and one line about it, a section for what they offer (a list or a menu), an about section, an image, a contact section with a real form (name, message, a way to reach them), and a footer.
  • Every section in the right semantic tag, every image with alt text, every input with a matching label.

Done when

  • The page uses header, nav, main, section, and footer, not a pile of divs.
  • Every image has meaningful alt text.
  • Every form input has a label tied to it.
  • The page passes the W3C validator with zero errors.

Stretch goal: Add a second page (a full menu or a services page) and link the two together in the nav.

Start lesson 2.1 →

docs.title: 2.1 HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
docs.text: Every website you have ever used, from Daraz to Google, is built from just three languages. Before you write any code, it helps to know what those three are, why the browser loves them, and where everything else you have heard of fits in.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• The three languages of the web, and what each one does
  • Why the browser only really speaks these three
  • Where other languages and big frameworks fit in
  • What a tag is, and why we mark up text instead of typing it plainly

docs.title: The three languages of the web
docs.text:
A web page is built in three layers, and each layer has its own language.

Three panels showing the three web languages: <abbr title="HyperText Markup Language, the language that gives a web page its structure and content. (Roman Urdu: web page ki structure banane wali zaban)">HTML</abbr> is structure, like the walls of a house; <abbr title="Cascading Style Sheets, the language that styles a page: colours, fonts, and layout. (Roman Urdu: page ko khoobsurat banane wali zaban)">CSS</abbr> is style, like the paint; <abbr title="The language that adds behaviour to a page, like reacting to clicks and typing. (Roman Urdu: page ko harkat aur amal dene wali zaban)">JavaScript</abbr> is behaviour, like the switches that make things work. HTML Structure the walls and rooms CSS Style the paint and decoration JavaScript Behaviour the switches that do things

Think of a house. HTML builds the walls and rooms, CSS paints and decorates them, and JavaScript adds the switches and doors that actually do something.
  • HTML (Roman Urdu: page ki structure aur content) gives the page its structure and content: headings, paragraphs, images, buttons. This whole chapter is about HTML.
  • CSS (Roman Urdu: page ki shakl aur rang) sets the look: colours, fonts, spacing, and layout. That is Chapter 3.
  • JavaScript (Roman Urdu: page ka amal aur harkat) adds behaviour: things that happen when you click, type, or scroll. That starts in Chapter 7.
Language Its job Without it you get HTML structure and content nothing, a blank page CSS colours, fonts, layout plain black text on white JavaScript behaviour and interaction a page that cannot react

docs.title: Why only these three?
docs.text:
A browser like Chrome is built to understand exactly these three languages and nothing else. They are the native tongue of the web. Whatever you build, however you build it, it has to reach the browser as HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. That is why they are the foundation of everything here.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ویب کی تین زبانیں ہیں: HTML صفحے کی ساخت بناتی ہے، CSS اسے خوبصورت بناتی ہے، اور JavaScript اسے حرکت اور عمل دیتی ہے۔ براؤزر صرف انہی تین زبانوں کو سمجھتا ہے۔ آپ چاہے کوئی بھی ٹول استعمال کریں، آخر میں سب کچھ انہی تین میں بدل کر براؤزر تک پہنچتا ہے۔

docs.title: Then what are Python, Java, and the rest?
docs.text:
You may have heard of Python, Java, or C++. These are real and powerful, but they are for different jobs. Languages split roughly by where they run.

Language Mainly used for HTML, CSS, JavaScript the frontend, inside the browser Python servers, data, AI, and scripts Java, C# large apps, Android, business systems C, C++ operating systems, games, hardware PHP the backend of many older websites

So if you want to build a Windows program or train an AI model, you reach for a different language. To build the part of a website a person sees and clicks, you reach for HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

docs.title: What about React, Django, and frameworks?
docs.text:
Soon you will hear names like React, Vue, Next.js, Django, and Laravel. These are frameworks and libraries: ready-made toolkits that make building faster. But here is the part that surprises people.

Frameworks like React and Django all end up producing <abbr title="HyperText Markup Language, the language that gives a web page its structure and content. (Roman Urdu: web page ki structure banane wali zaban)">HTML</abbr>, <abbr title="Cascading Style Sheets, the language that styles a page: colours, fonts, and layout. (Roman Urdu: page ko khoobsurat banane wali zaban)">CSS</abbr>, and <abbr title="The language that adds behaviour to a page, like reacting to clicks and typing. (Roman Urdu: page ko harkat aur amal dene wali zaban)">JavaScript</abbr>, which is then sent to the browser. Frameworks React, Vue, Django, more HTML + CSS + JS what the browser reads The browser draws the page become sent to

No matter which framework you use, it all turns into HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before it reaches the browser. The browser speaks nothing else.

The browser still only understands HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. So every framework, in the end, produces those three for the browser. React is written in JavaScript and generates HTML. Django runs on the server and sends HTML, CSS, and JS back to the browser. The toolkit changes; the destination never does.

That is why learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript is never wasted. It is the ground under every framework you will ever touch. Start strong here, and the rest gets easier.

docs.title: What is a tag, and why do we need it?
docs.text:
The browser cannot read your mind. If you simply type:

Welcome to my site

the browser shows grey text and has no idea what it is. Is it a big title? A button? A paragraph? It cannot tell. So you wrap the text in a tag, a small label in angle brackets that says what the text is:

<h1>Welcome to my site</h1>

Now the browser knows this is h1, the main heading, and shows it big and bold. That is the whole idea of HTML: plain text carries no meaning, and tags give it meaning.

Why can we not just type normally? Because the structure that tags add is what lets the browser show the right style, lets CSS decorate it, lets JavaScript find and change it, and lets Google and screen readers understand your page. Without tags, a page is just a flat wall of text.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open any website in Chrome, right-click something, and choose Inspect (the trick from lesson 1.4). In the Elements panel you will see that every single piece of the page is wrapped in tags. Find one <h1> and one <p>. That tree of tags is exactly the HTML you are about to learn to write yourself.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. What does each of the three web languages do?
  2. Why does the browser only need HTML, CSS, and JavaScript?
  3. React and Django are very different, but what do both eventually send to the browser?
  4. Why do we wrap text in tags instead of typing it plainly?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Now you have the big picture. Next you open VS Code and write your very first HTML page, first by hand so you understand every line, then with a shortcut that writes the skeleton for you.

Next lesson: 2.2 Your first HTML page →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • MDN: What is HTML? a gentle first look.
  • web.dev: Learn HTML, overview the bigger map of the language.

docs.title: 2.2 Your first HTML page
docs.text: Now you build a real page. First you will set up VS Code the right way, then write the page skeleton by hand so you understand every line, then learn the shortcut that writes it for you in one keystroke.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• How to open a project folder in VS Code, and why a folder
  • How to create files, and what index.html is
  • The VS Code integrated terminal, and how it differs from a normal one
  • The three parts of a tag, and what an attribute is
  • How to write the HTML boilerplate by hand, then with the emmet ! shortcut
  • What boilerplate is, and why nearly every language has some

docs.title: Start in a folder, not a file
docs.text:
Open VS Code, then choose File > Open Folder and pick (or make) a folder for your work, like banoqabil/chapter-2.

Why a folder and not just a file? Because a website is never one file. It is HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and images, all living together. VS Code is built to work on a whole folder, which it calls your project. With the folder open it can see every file at once, search across all of them, and run tools for you. Open a lone file and you lose all of that.

Trust the folder

Always open the folder, then create files inside it. This one habit prevents a surprising number of beginner headaches later.

docs.title: Create your first file
docs.text: In the Explorer on the left, hover your folder name and click the New File icon. Name the file index.html. Later you will add style.css and script.js next to it, and they will all be part of the same project.

docs.title: Why the name index.html?
docs.text: index.html is the default home page name on the web. When a browser opens a folder or a website address, it looks for index.html first and shows it without being told. It is the front door of your site. So your main page is almost always named index.html, and other pages get names like about.html or contact.html.

docs.title: The integrated terminal in VS Code
docs.text:
You met the terminal in lesson 1.3. VS Code has one built right in. Open it with View > Terminal, or the shortcut Ctrl + `.

It runs the very same shell as your normal terminal (on Windows it can run PowerShell or your WSL bash). The difference is small but genuinely useful:

  • The integrated terminal opens already inside your project folder, so you are in the right place from the start.
  • It sits right under your code, so you do not switch windows to run a command and read its output.
  • When an error prints a file path, you can click it and VS Code jumps to that file and line.

A normal terminal is a separate app that starts in some default folder. The integrated one is the same tool, just living inside your editor and tied to your project. Same commands, far less switching back and forth.

docs.title: The anatomy of a tag
docs.text:
Most tags come in pairs: an opening tag, your content, then a closing tag. The closing tag has a slash before its name.

<p>Hello</p>
  • <p> is the opening tag. It starts a paragraph.
  • Hello is the content. This is what the user reads.
  • </p> is the closing tag. The slash means "this paragraph ends here."

An opening tag, its content, and its closing tag together make one element.

Sometimes a tag needs extra information. You add it with an attribute inside the opening tag, in the form name="value".

<html lang="en">

Here lang is the attribute name and "en" is its value, telling the browser the page is in English.

docs.title: Write the skeleton by hand
docs.text:
Type this into index.html yourself, line by line. Typing it once by hand is how you learn what each line is for.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>My first page</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1>Hello, world</h1>
    <p>This is my first web page.</p>
  </body>
</html>

Read it line by line:

  • <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser this is a modern HTML page.
  • <html lang="en"> is the root tag. Everything else goes inside it.
  • <head> holds information about the page, not shown on screen.
  • <meta charset="UTF-8"> sets the text encoding, so every letter and symbol shows correctly.
  • <meta name="viewport" ...> makes the page fit nicely on phones.
  • <title> is the page name shown on the browser tab.
  • <body> holds the content users actually see.
  • <h1> and <p> are the heading and paragraph inside the body.

docs.title: The faster way: emmet and the !
docs.text:
Typing that whole skeleton every time would be slow. VS Code ships with Emmet, a shortcut system that expands short abbreviations into full code.

Try it. In an empty .html file, type a single exclamation mark and press Tab:

!

Emmet instantly writes the entire skeleton above for you. One character, a full starting page. You will meet more Emmet shortcuts in a later lesson, where one short line expands into many tags.

Hand first, shortcut after

Write the skeleton by hand at least once so you understand it. After that, use ! every time. Understanding first, speed second.

docs.title: What is boilerplate?
docs.text:
That skeleton is called boilerplate: the standard starting code that is almost the same in every file of its kind. Every HTML page needs this same opening structure, so it is boilerplate.

Does every language have boilerplate? Most do. A Python script, a React app, a C program: each has a small, standard block you write to begin. We write it (or let a tool like Emmet generate it) because the language and the browser expect that structure before anything else will work properly. The boilerplate sets up encoding, mobile scaling, and the title, so everything you add afterwards behaves.

docs.title: Headings, paragraphs, and comments
docs.text:
HTML gives you six heading levels, <h1> down to <h6>. Use one <h1> per page as the main title, like the name of a book. The smaller ones break content into sections. The <p> tag holds normal blocks of text.

<h1>This is the main title</h1>
<h2>This is a section title</h2>
<p>This is normal paragraph text.</p>

A comment is a note for you, not the user. The browser ignores it. You write one between <!-- and -->.

<!-- This is a comment. The browser will not show it. -->
<p>This paragraph is visible.</p>
اردو میں مزید وضاحت

HTML کا ہر صفحہ ایک ہی جیسے ڈھانچے سے شروع ہوتا ہے جسے boilerplate کہتے ہیں۔ اسے ایک بار خود ہاتھ سے لکھیں تاکہ سمجھ آ جائے، پھر VS Code میں صرف ! لکھ کر Tab دبائیں اور Emmet پورا ڈھانچہ خود لکھ دے گا۔ صفحہ دو حصوں میں ہوتا ہے: head معلومات کے لیے، اور body اس مواد کے لیے جو صارف دیکھتا ہے۔

docs.title: How the browser reads your HTML
docs.text:
The browser reads your file top to bottom and builds a tree of elements. The <html> element sits at the top. Inside it sit <head> and <body>, and inside those sit more elements.

An HTML document as a tree: html contains <abbr title="The part of the page that holds information about the page and is not shown to users. (Roman Urdu: page ki maloomat wala hissa jo screen par nazar nahi aata)">head</abbr> and <abbr title="The part of the page that holds the content users see on screen. (Roman Urdu: page ka wo hissa jo user screen par dekhta hai)">body</abbr>; <abbr title="The part of the page that holds information about the page and is not shown to users. (Roman Urdu: page ki maloomat wala hissa jo screen par nazar nahi aata)">head</abbr> contains title; <abbr title="The part of the page that holds the content users see on screen. (Roman Urdu: page ka wo hissa jo user screen par dekhta hai)">body</abbr> contains an h1 and a p. <html> <head> <body> <title> <h1> <p>

The browser turns your HTML into a tree. The html element holds head and body, and each of those holds more elements.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text:
1. In VS Code, open your chapter-2 folder with File > Open Folder.
  2. Create a new file named index.html.
  3. In the empty file, type ! and press Tab to drop in the skeleton.
  4. Change the <title> to My first page and the <h1> to your own name, like Hello, I am Ayesha.
  5. Save with Ctrl + S. Then right-click the file and open it in your browser, or use the Live Server extension and click "Go Live".

You should see your heading in the browser. Change the <h1>, save, and refresh: only the part you changed moves. That is the loop you will repeat thousands of times.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's exactly what this section is for.

  1. Why do you open a folder in VS Code instead of a single file?
  2. Why is the main page usually named index.html?
  3. What does typing ! and pressing Tab do in an HTML file?
  4. What is boilerplate, and why do we write it?
  5. What is the difference between the <head> and the <body>?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You can build a basic page and you understand its skeleton. Next you fill it with real content: formatted text, lists, links to other pages, and images.

Next lesson: 2.3 Text, lists, links, and images →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • web.dev: Learn HTML, overview
  • MDN: HTML basics
  • Emmet: Emmet cheat sheet every abbreviation, for later.

docs.title: 2.3 Text, lists, links, and images
docs.text: In lesson 2.2 you built your first HTML page with headings and paragraphs. Now you will give that text real meaning and structure. You will mark words as important, build neat lists, add links to other pages, and show images. By the end your page will feel like a real web page, not just a wall of text.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• How to mark text as important with <strong> and stressed with <em>.
  • How to build ordered and unordered lists, and when to pick each one.
  • How to link to other websites and to your own files.
  • How to add images with good alt text and a fixed size.
  • The difference between block elements and inline elements.

docs.title: Text emphasis: strong and em
docs.text:
Sometimes a word in your text matters more than the rest. HTML has two tags for this.

<p>This is <strong>very important</strong> to read.</p>
<p>I said the <em>blue</em> button, not the red one.</p>

<strong> means the text is important. The browser usually shows it in bold. <em> means you stress the word when you speak it. The browser usually shows it in italic.

The key idea is meaning, not looks. These tags tell the browser and screen readers that the word carries weight. Do not use them just to make text bold or italic for style. You will learn the styling way (CSS) in a later chapter.

docs.title: Line breaks: use them sparingly
docs.text:
A <br> tag forces a line break inside text. It has no closing tag.

<p>Roses are red,<br>violets are blue.</p>

This is useful for things like a postal address or a short poem. Do not use <br> to create space between paragraphs. For separate ideas, use separate <p> tags instead. Spacing is a job for CSS, not for stacking many <br> tags.

docs.title: Lists: ordered and unordered
docs.text:
Lists turn a group of items into a clean, readable block.

An unordered list uses <ul>. Each item sits inside an <li> tag. The browser shows a bullet point.

<ul>
  <li>HTML</li>
  <li>CSS</li>
  <li>JavaScript</li>
</ul>

An ordered list uses <ol>. It also uses <li> for each item. The browser shows numbers instead of bullets.

<ol>
  <li>Open your editor</li>
  <li>Write the HTML</li>
  <li>Open the file in a browser</li>
</ol>

Pick based on meaning. Use <ul> when the order does not matter, like a list of skills. Use <ol> when the order matters, like the steps in a recipe.

docs.title: Links: the anchor tag
docs.text:
A link uses the anchor tag <a>. The href attribute holds the address you want to go to.

<a href="https://banoqabil.pk">Visit Bano Qabil</a>
<a href="about.html">About us</a>

The first link uses an absolute URL. That is the full address, starting with https://, to another website. Use it when you link outside your own site.

The second link uses a relative URL. That points to a file inside your own folder. Here about.html sits next to your current file. Use relative URLs to link between your own pages.

You can also open a link in a new browser tab. Add target="_blank".

<a href="https://banoqabil.pk" target="_blank">Open in a new tab</a>

This is nice for outside links, so the reader does not leave your page.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

لنک کے دو طریقے ہوتے ہیں۔ مطلق پتہ (absolute URL) پوری ایڈریس ہوتی ہے جو https سے شروع ہوتی ہے اور کسی دوسری ویب سائٹ کی طرف لے جاتی ہے۔ متعلقہ پتہ (relative URL) صرف فائل کا نام ہوتا ہے جو آپ کے اپنے فولڈر میں موجود ہے، جیسے about.html۔ جب آپ اپنی ویب سائٹ کے صفحات کو آپس میں جوڑیں تو متعلقہ پتہ استعمال کریں۔ جب کسی باہر کی سائٹ کا لنک دیں تو مطلق پتہ استعمال کریں۔ اگر فائل کا راستہ غلط ہو تو لنک کام نہیں کرے گا۔

Common mistake: broken relative paths

If your file is in a different folder, a plain name like about.html will not work. The browser looks for that file next to the current page. Always check that the file really sits where your href points.

docs.title: Images: the img tag
docs.text:
An image uses the <img> tag. It has no closing tag. The src attribute holds the path to the image file.

<img src="team-photo.jpg" alt="Our team standing in front of the office" width="600" height="400">

The src works like an href. It can be a relative path to a file in your folder, or a full URL to an image online.

The alt attribute holds text that describes the image. This text matters for three reasons. Screen readers read it aloud for users who cannot see. The browser shows it if the image fails to load. Search engines read it to understand your page.
