State What it means isLoading isError data pending First fetch in progress, no cache yet true false undefined error Fetch failed and there is no cached copy false true undefined success Data is available, either fresh or cached false false your data stale Data was fetched before, but it is old enough to refetch false false your data (old copy shown while refetch runs)

The stale state is unique to server data. Your users see the old data instantly, and the fresh copy arrives in the background. No blank screen.

docs.title: The cache flow
docs.text:
Here is how a request actually moves through TanStack Query. Two paths: cache hit and cache miss.

<abbr title="A library that manages server state for you, handling caching, refetching, loading, and error states. (Roman Urdu: server state sambhalne wali library)">TanStack Query</abbr> cache flow. A component calls <abbr title="A TanStack Query hook that fetches and caches data, giving you data, isLoading, and isError. (Roman Urdu: data fetch aur cache karne wala hook)">useQuery</abbr>. <abbr title="A library that manages server state for you, handling caching, refetching, loading, and error states. (Roman Urdu: server state sambhalne wali library)">TanStack Query</abbr> checks the cache. On a hit it returns the cached data immediately and then refetches in the background to keep it fresh. On a miss it fetches from the server, stores the result in the cache, and then returns the data. Component calls useQuery Check cache for queryKey Cache HIT return data now Cache MISS fetch from server Store in cache return data ...then silently refetch in background

On a cache hit the user sees data instantly. TanStack Query then refetches silently in the background to keep the data fresh. On a cache miss it fetches, stores the result, then returns it.

docs.title: Caching and revalidation
docs.text:
Your data is cached by its queryKey. So ["products"] is one cache entry. When you ask for it again, you get the cached copy at once. No waiting.

But remember, server state can go stale. So TanStack Query does something clever. It shows you the cached data right away. Then it quietly refetches in the background. If the new data is different, your screen updates. The user sees something instantly, and it stays fresh.

You can also fetch a single item using an id in the key. Each product gets its own cache entry.

function ProductDetail({ id }) {
  const { data, isLoading } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["products", id],   // each id is a separate cache slot
    queryFn: () =>
      fetch(`https://fakestoreapi.com/products/${id}`).then((r) => r.json()),
  });

  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
  return <h2>{data.title}</h2>;
}

Did you know

TanStack Query refetches stale data in the background. So your users see cached content instantly while the fresh copy arrives behind the scenes. They never stare at a blank loading screen for data they have seen before.

docs.title: Changing data with useMutation
docs.text:
useQuery is for reading. To change data on the server, you use useMutation. This covers create, update, and delete.

After you change data, the cached list is now wrong. You added a product, but the old list does not show it. So you invalidate the query. That tells TanStack Query to refetch it.

import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";

function AddProduct() {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  const mutation = useMutation({
    mutationFn: (newProduct) =>
      fetch("https://fakestoreapi.com/products", {
        method: "POST",
        body: JSON.stringify(newProduct),
      }).then((res) => res.json()),
    onSuccess: () => {
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["products"] });
    },
  });

  return (
    <button onClick={() => mutation.mutate({ title: "New shirt" })}>
      Add product
    </button>
  );
}

The mutationFn sends the change to the server. When it succeeds, onSuccess runs. There you call invalidateQueries with the ["products"] key. That marks the list as stale, so TanStack Query refetches it. Your list updates with the new product automatically.

docs.title: Pagination and infinite scroll
docs.text:
Sometimes a list is too big to load all at once. Think of an endless feed of posts.

For this, TanStack Query has useInfiniteQuery. It handles loading data page by page. You give it a way to find the next page. It tracks all the pages for you. This is how you build "load more" buttons and infinite scroll. You do not need to memorize it now. Just know the tool exists for that job.

docs.title: Using it with Next.js
docs.text:
TanStack Query works inside client components. So any component with "use client" at the top can use useQuery and useMutation. That is the normal place for it.

Server components are different. As you saw in 14.3, a server component can fetch data directly. It runs on the server, so it just awaits the fetch. You do not need TanStack Query there. Use TanStack Query when fetching happens in the browser, after the page loads.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ریاست کی دو قسمیں ہیں۔ UI ریاست آپ کی اپنی ہے، جیسے کوئی modal کھلا ہے یا بند۔ Server ریاست کسی دور دراز server پر رہتی ہے اور آپ صرف اس کی نقل ادھار لیتے ہیں جو پرانی ہو سکتی ہے۔ TanStack Query آپ کے لیے یہ server ریاست سنبھالتی ہے: cache میں ڈیٹا رکھتی ہے، پرانا ہونے پر خود بخود دوبارہ fetch کرتی ہے، اور loading اور error کی حالتیں دیتی ہے۔ جب data cache میں ہو تو user کو فوری نظر آتا ہے جبکہ نیا data پس پردہ آتا رہتا ہے۔ Data بدلنے کے لیے useMutation استعمال ہوتا ہے اور کامیابی پر queryKey کو invalidate کر کے list دوبارہ fetch کی جاتی ہے۔

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Take a component that fetches data with useEffect and rewrite it with useQuery. Wrap your app in a QueryClientProvider, then fetch the products from https://fakestoreapi.com/products using a queryKey of ["products"]. Show the isLoading and isError states, then list the titles. Open the page, leave it, and come back. Notice how the second visit shows the data instantly from the cache instead of a loading spinner.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. What is the difference between UI state and server state? Give one example of each.
  2. Which two things does useQuery need to fetch data?
  3. After a useMutation succeeds, what do you call so the list refetches?
  4. Why does TanStack Query show cached data first and then refetch in the background?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You now manage server state cleanly. Your app fetches, caches, and updates data with very little code. Next you will make sure your app feels fast for real users. In 18.3 you will look at performance and Core Web Vitals.

Next lesson: 18.3 Performance and Core Web Vitals →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • TanStack Query Overview
  • TanStack Query Queries

docs.title: 19.3 Performance and Core Web Vitals
docs.text: Most of your users open your site on a budget phone, on a slow data connection. Every megabyte you send costs them money and time. A heavy page loads slowly, jumps around, and feels broken even when the code is correct. In this lesson you learn how to measure speed and fix the worst problems.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• Why performance matters more for users in Pakistan.
  • How to run Lighthouse in Chrome and read its four scores.
  • What LCP, INP, and CLS mean in plain words.
  • How to size, format, and lazy-load images so they load fast.
  • How to load fonts and JavaScript so the page shows quickly.
  • How to run Lighthouse on your Chapter 13 capstone and fix the top issues.

docs.title: Why speed matters here
docs.text:
Many of your users are on a slow 3G or weak 4G signal. Their phones have small memory and slow processors. Data also costs money, so a fat page wastes their rupees.

A slow page makes people leave before it even loads. If your shop is slow, the user buys nothing. So speed is not a bonus. It is part of the product.

The good news is that small fixes give big wins. You do not need a faster phone. You need a lighter page.

docs.title: Running Lighthouse
docs.text:
You met Lighthouse back in Lesson 13.4. Here is the quick recall.

Open your site in Chrome. Press F12 to open DevTools. Click the Lighthouse tab. Choose Mobile as the device. Then click Analyze page load.

Lighthouse gives you four scores out of 100:

  • Performance: how fast the page loads and responds.
  • Accessibility: how usable it is for everyone.
  • Best Practices: safe and correct coding habits.
  • SEO: how well search engines can read it.

Aim for green (90 and above) on each. Red means something needs work now. Lighthouse also lists exact problems with each one, so read that list.

Tip

Always test in Mobile mode with throttling on. Throttling slows the connection to copy a real phone on slow data. That is much closer to your actual Pakistani user than your fast laptop on Wi-Fi.

docs.title: The three Core Web Vitals
docs.text:
Google measures three numbers to judge real user experience. They are called Core Web Vitals. Learn these three names.

Vital Full name What it measures Good target What hurts it most LCP Largest Contentful Paint How fast the biggest thing on screen shows up Under 2.5 s Large unoptimized images, render-blocking scripts INP Interaction to Next Paint How fast the page reacts to a tap or click Under 200 ms Heavy JavaScript running on every user action CLS Cumulative Layout Shift How much the page jumps around while loading Under 0.1 Images with no width/height, late-loading ads or fonts

LCP is about loading. The biggest item is usually a hero image or a big heading. If it shows in under 2.5 seconds, the user feels the page is fast.

INP is about responding. When the user taps a button, the page should react in under 200 milliseconds. A slow reaction feels frozen.

CLS is about stability. You have seen a page where you go to tap a link, then an image loads above it and pushes everything down. That jump is layout shift. Keep it under 0.1.

docs.title: What a loading timeline looks like
docs.text:
The diagram below shows a simplified page load timeline. The key event is LCP: the moment the biggest thing on screen is fully painted.

A horizontal page load timeline from 0 to 4 seconds. The HTML arrives near 0s. CSS and fonts arrive by 0.8s. The first content paint happens around 1s. The <abbr title="Largest Contentful Paint, how fast the biggest item on screen shows up. (Roman Urdu: sab se bari cheez kitni jaldi nazar aati hai)">LCP</abbr>, the largest image, paints at 2.1s which is inside the good target of 2.5s. At 3s an ad banner loads late and causes a layout shift, pushing <abbr title="Cumulative Layout Shift, how much the page jumps around while loading. (Roman Urdu: page load hote waqt kitna uchalta hai)">CLS</abbr> above the good threshold. 0 s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 s HTML CSS + Fonts JS Hero image (LCP element) LCP: 2.1 s (good, under 2.5 s) Late ad loads CLS jumps

A simplified load timeline. The hero image paints at 2.1 s, inside the 2.5 s LCP target. A late-loading ad at 3 s causes a layout shift, hurting CLS. Fix the ad by reserving space for it, and fix a slow LCP by optimizing the hero image. اردو میں مزید وضاحت

Core Web Vitals تین اہم پیمائشیں ہیں جو صفحے کا تجربہ ناپتی ہیں۔ LCP بتاتا ہے کہ سب سے بڑی چیز، عموماً ہیرو تصویر، کتنی جلدی نظر آتی ہے، اور یہ ڈھائی سیکنڈ سے کم ہونی چاہیے۔ INP ناپتا ہے کہ صفحہ آپ کے ٹچ کا کتنی جلدی جواب دیتا ہے، اور یہ دو سو ملی سیکنڈ سے کم ہونا چاہیے۔ CLS بتاتا ہے کہ صفحہ لوڈ ہوتے وقت کتنا اچھلتا ہے، جیسے تصویر بغیر سائز کے لوڈ ہو اور نیچے کا مواد نیچے چلا جائے۔ تصویر پر width اور height لگانا CLS کا سب سے آسان علاج ہے۔

docs.title: Images done right
docs.text:
Images are usually the heaviest part of a page. You saw image basics in Lessons 3.4 and 14.4. Here is the performance view.

Size the image to the space it fills. Do not load a 4000 pixel wide photo into a 400 pixel box. Resize it first.

Always set width and height on the image. This reserves the space before the image loads, so the page does not jump. That protects your CLS score.

// Bad: no size, so the page jumps when this loads
<img src="/shoe.jpg" alt="Running shoe" />

// Good: space is reserved, no layout shift
<img src="/shoe.jpg" alt="Running shoe" width="400" height="300" />

Use modern formats like WebP or AVIF. They are much smaller than old JPG or PNG for the same quality.

Lazy-load images that are below the fold, meaning the ones the user must scroll to see. They load only when needed, so the first screen is fast.

// Lazy-load an offscreen product image
<img
  src="/product-12.webp"
  alt="Blue jacket"
  width="300"
  height="300"
  loading="lazy"
/>

If you use Next.js, the next/image component does most of this for you. It sets sizes, picks modern formats, and lazy-loads automatically.

Warning

A heavy hero image or an image with no width and height is the most common cause of a bad LCP and a bad CLS. Fix your big images first. You will see the score jump.

docs.title: Loading fonts without the jump
docs.text:
Custom fonts take time to download. While they load, the browser may hide your text or swap it in suddenly. Both feel bad.

Use font-display: swap. This shows your text in a normal fallback font right away. When the real font arrives, it swaps in. The user always sees words, never a blank space.

@font-face {
  font-family: "Sora";
  src: url("/fonts/sora.woff2") format("woff2");
  font-display: swap;
}

This keeps text visible during load and helps avoid layout shift, which protects CLS.

docs.title: Code splitting
docs.text:
Your JavaScript also has a size. If you send all of it on the first load, even pages that do not need it, the start is slow.

Code splitting means you load only the JavaScript a page needs right now. You load the rest later, when the user actually goes there.

In plain JavaScript you use a dynamic import.

// Load the chart code only when the user opens the report
button.addEventListener("click", async () => {
  const { drawChart } = await import("./chart.js");
  drawChart();
});

In React you use React.lazy to split a component into its own chunk.

import { lazy, Suspense } from "react";

// This heavy page loads only when someone visits it
const Checkout = lazy(() => import("./Checkout.jsx"));

function App() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading...</p>}>
      <Checkout />
    </Suspense>
  );
}

The first load stays small, so the user sees something fast.

docs.title: Bundle analysis
docs.text:
Sometimes your JavaScript is big and you do not know why. A bundle analyzer shows you. It draws a map of every library inside your build, sized by how much space it takes.

# One common analyzer for Vite projects
npm install --save-dev rollup-plugin-visualizer

After you build, it opens a chart. You might find one chart library or one date library eating half your bundle. Then you can replace it with a smaller one or remove it. You only fix what you can see, so measure first.

docs.title: Performance fixes at a glance
docs.text:
Here is a checklist you can run on any project. If a row is failing in Lighthouse, use the fix column.

Problem Which metric it hurts Fix Hero image is a large JPG, not compressed LCP Convert to WebP, resize to the display size Images have no width or height CLS Add both attributes to every <img> All images load on page start LCP (first paint is slow) Add loading="lazy" to below-the-fold images A big library is imported for one small job LCP (large JS bundle) Replace with a small helper or plain JS All routes share one big JS file LCP Use React.lazy + Suspense to split routes Custom font hides text while downloading CLS, perceived speed Add font-display: swap to your @font-face Heavy event handler runs on every keystroke INP Debounce the handler or move work off the main thread Third-party scripts load early LCP Load them with async or defer

docs.title: Hands-on: speed up your Chapter 13 capstone
docs.text:
Open your Chapter 13 ecommerce capstone. Run Lighthouse in Mobile mode with throttling on. Read the top problems. You will likely see these three.

1. Images have no dimensions. Your product images probably have no width and height. Add them. This stops the page from jumping and helps both LCP and CLS.

<img src={product.image} alt={product.name} width="300" height="300" />

2. All images load at once. Your product grid loads every image, even the ones far down the page. Add loading="lazy" to images below the first screen.

<img src={product.image} alt={product.name} width="300" height="300" loading="lazy" />

3. A heavy library bloats the bundle. Maybe you pulled in a big library for one small job, like a full date library to show one date. Swap it for a tiny helper or plain JavaScript. Smaller bundle, faster first load.

Run Lighthouse again after each fix. Watch the score climb. That feedback loop is the whole game.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open any site you have built in Chrome, press F12, and run Lighthouse in Mobile mode with throttling on. Write down your four scores. Then pick the single biggest problem it lists, often an image with no width and height, fix just that one thing, and run Lighthouse again. Compare the before and after scores so you can see the win from one small change.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. What do LCP, INP, and CLS each measure, and what is a good target for each?
  2. Why does setting width and height on an image help your CLS score?
  3. What does font-display: swap do while a custom font is still loading?
  4. What problem does code splitting with React.lazy solve?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You can now measure your site and fix the worst speed problems. The last step before launch is making sure your project is clean and safe to ship. In 18.4 you learn production hygiene, like checking environment variables, removing console logs, and a final pre-launch checklist.

Next lesson: 18.4 Production hygiene →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • web.dev: Web Vitals
  • Chrome: Lighthouse overview

docs.title: 19.4 Production hygiene
docs.text: Your app is live and real people can use it. Now you need to keep it safe, fast, and easy to fix. Production hygiene means the small habits that protect your secrets and tell you when things break. This lesson gives you simple checklists you can run before every launch.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• Store secrets in environment variables, not in your code or Git.
  • Understand why the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix exposes a value to the browser.
  • Add error monitoring with Sentry so you hear about bugs first.
  • Add privacy-friendly analytics with one line of code.
  • Run an SEO checklist and an accessibility checklist on each page.
  • Use a "ready to launch" checklist before you share your app.

docs.title: Environment variables done right
docs.text:
A secret (Roman Urdu: woh cheez jo chhupani ho) is a value you must hide. Think of an API key or a database password. You learned in 5.1 to keep files out of Git with .gitignore. Secrets follow the same rule. They never go inside your code, and they never go into Git.

Instead, you store them as environment variables. On your computer you put them in a .env file. That file is listed in .gitignore, so Git ignores it.

# .env  (this file is in .gitignore, so Git never sees it)
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@host/db
SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN=your-secret-token

For production, you do not upload the .env file. You set the same names in your host dashboard. On Vercel or Netlify you open Settings, then Environment Variables, and add each one. The host keeps them safe and feeds them to your app at build time.

Keep separate values for development and production. Your local app can talk to a test database. Your live app talks to the real one. Mixing them is how a student deletes real user data while testing.

docs.title: What goes in environment variables
docs.text:
Not every variable is a secret, but it helps to know which is which before you start.

Variable type Example name Example value Keep secret? Use NEXT_PUBLIC_? Database connection DATABASE_URL postgres://... Yes No Third-party API key STRIPE_SECRET_KEY sk_live_... Yes No Public site URL NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL https://myapp.com No Yes Analytics write key (public) NEXT_PUBLIC_ANALYTICS_KEY abc123 No Yes Email service key RESEND_API_KEY re_... Yes No Dev flag NODE_ENV development No No (set by host)

docs.title: The NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix
docs.text:
Some values are not secret. A public site URL is fine to show. In a Next.js app, any variable starting with NEXT_PUBLIC_ gets sent to the browser. That means anyone can read it.

// Safe: this is meant to be public
const siteUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL;

// Server only: no prefix, so it stays hidden on the server
const dbUrl = process.env.DATABASE_URL;

Warning

Anything with the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix is visible to everyone who opens your site. Never put a real secret key behind that prefix. Anyone can open the browser tools and read it in seconds.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

سیکرٹ ویلیوز جیسے پاس ورڈ یا API key کبھی بھی کوڈ یا Git میں نہ رکھیں۔ انہیں environment variables میں رکھیں اور لائیو سائٹ پر host کے dashboard میں ڈالیں۔ Next.js میں جس variable کے شروع میں NEXT_PUBLIC_ لگا ہو وہ browser تک پہنچ جاتا ہے، یعنی ہر کوئی اسے دیکھ سکتا ہے، اس لیے کوئی اصلی secret اس prefix کے ساتھ کبھی نہ رکھیں۔ Development اور production کے لیے الگ الگ values رکھیں تاکہ testing میں اصلی data خراب نہ ہو۔ یہ چھوٹی سی احتیاط بڑے حادثے سے بچاتی ہے۔

docs.title: Error monitoring with Sentry
docs.text:
When a bug hits a real user, the user rarely tells you. They just leave. Error monitoring (Roman Urdu: errors ko pakarne wala system) fixes that gap. It catches errors that happen on real devices and reports them to you. You get the stack trace, the page, and the browser, so you can find and fix the bug fast.

The diagram below shows the full path from a user hitting a bug to you getting the alert.

Error monitoring flow. A user on their phone hits a JavaScript error. <abbr title="A popular error monitoring service with a free tier for small projects. (Roman Urdu: ek mashhoor error monitoring service jiska free plan hai)">Sentry</abbr> captures it automatically. <abbr title="A popular error monitoring service with a free tier for small projects. (Roman Urdu: ek mashhoor error monitoring service jiska free plan hai)">Sentry</abbr> sends you an email alert with the stack trace, the page URL, and the browser. You fix the bug and deploy. User hits a bug on their phone Sentry captures the error You get an alert: stack trace, page, browser You fix and deploy Without monitoring, the user just leaves and you never find out.

Sentry sits between the user's device and your inbox. A bug that would silently drive users away becomes a clear report you can act on.

Sentry has a free tier that is plenty for a student project. You install it and wrap your app once.

npm install @sentry/nextjs
npx @sentry/wizard@latest -i nextjs

The wizard adds the setup files for you. After that, Sentry watches your app and sends you an email when something breaks.

Tip

Add error monitoring before you launch, not after. That way your first bug report comes from Sentry with a clear stack trace, not from an angry user with a vague "it does not work" message.

docs.title: Analytics that respect privacy
docs.text:
Analytics (Roman Urdu: maaloomat ke kaun kaun se log aaye) tell you which pages people visit and how many came by. You do not need heavy trackers that follow users around. Privacy-friendly tools like Plausible or Vercel Analytics give you the numbers without all that.

Vercel Analytics turns on with one small piece of code in your app layout.

import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/react";

export default function Layout({ children }) {
  return (
    <>
      {children}
      <Analytics />
    </>
  );
}

Now you can see your most popular pages in the dashboard. This helps you decide what to improve next.

docs.title: SEO checklist
docs.text:
Good SEO helps people find your site through search. You met titles and meta tags in 2.4, and performance in 14.4. Run this list on each page.

  • A clear, unique title for every page.
  • A short meta description that says what the page is about.
  • Semantic HTML, so headings and sections have real meaning.
  • Alt text on every image.
  • A sitemap.xml and a robots.txt file.
  • Fast load time, since slow pages rank lower.

docs.title: Accessibility checklist
docs.text:
Accessibility means everyone can use your site, including people with disabilities. You covered the basics in 2.4. Check these before launch.

  • Keyboard navigation works, so you can reach everything without a mouse.
  • Focus is visible, so users can see where they are.
  • Alt text describes every meaningful image.
  • Inputs have labels, so screen readers can name them.
  • Color contrast is strong enough to read.
  • Headings follow order, from h1 to h2 to h3, with no jumps.

docs.title: The "ready to launch" checklist
docs.text:
Run this final list right before you tell people about your app. Tick every box before you share the link.

Check How to verify Pass condition Works on mobile Open on your phone (not just DevTools) No broken layout, text readable, buttons tappable Lighthouse score Run in Mobile + throttling mode Performance, Accessibility, SEO all 90+ No console errors Open DevTools on the live URL, check Console tab Zero red errors Secrets are out of Git Check .gitignore for .env; search repo for raw keys No keys in any committed file Error monitoring is live Trigger a test error, confirm Sentry email arrives Alert received Analytics are on Open the analytics dashboard after a page visit Visit recorded Custom domain with HTTPS Open the site URL in browser Padlock icon visible, https:// in address bar README explains the project Open the repo root README is clear to someone who has not seen the project No console.log left in production code Grep the source files Clean output

Note

You will not tick every box on your very first project. That is fine. The list exists so you know what "done" looks like. Over time it becomes a habit, not a chore.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Pick one project you have deployed and run the "ready to launch" checklist on it. Move any secret into a .env file and confirm that file is listed in .gitignore. Open the live site on your phone, check the browser console for errors, and tick off each box on the list. Note down anything that fails so you have a short fix list before you share the link.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. Where do you store secrets for a live app, and why not in Git?
  2. What does the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix do to a variable?
  3. What problem does error monitoring solve for you?
  4. Name three items from the accessibility checklist.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Chapter 19 is done. You can now manage state and data, and ship a clean production app that is safe and watched. The final chapter helps you build a strong portfolio and land your first job.

Next chapter: 20. Career and portfolio capstone →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Sentry for JavaScript
  • Vercel Analytics

docs.title: Chapter 20: Career and portfolio capstone
docs.text:
Muhammad Umar Sajid · Trainer at Bano Qabil Sahiwal · Front End Development and Graphic Design

You can build real, fast, production-ready websites now. This final chapter turns that skill into a career. You will build a portfolio that gets you hired, polish your projects so they shine, and learn how to find your first paying clients.

This is the bridge from learning to earning. Take it seriously. A good portfolio and a clear plan are what turn months of study into a real income, the halal way.

docs.title: Lessons in this chapter
docs.text:
• 20.1 Planning a portfolio that gets you hired: what a strong junior portfolio looks like in 2026.
  • 20.2 Build your portfolio: build it with everything you have learned.
  • 20.3 Polish and the README that wins interviews: make each project easy to understand and trust.
  • 20.4 First clients and freelancing in Pakistan: find work and earn on the halal path.

Done beats perfect

A finished, deployed portfolio with three real projects beats a perfect one you never ship. Build it, put it online, and improve it over time.

docs.title: Mega assignment
docs.text:
Ship your portfolio and go after real work

This is the assignment the whole course was building toward. Turn your study into something an employer or a client can hire you from, then actually reach out. Done and sent beats perfect and hidden.

What you build and do

  • A deployed portfolio site with at least three real projects: the client site you have grown since Chapter 1, the ecommerce store, and one more of your choice.
  • For each project: a live link, the source on GitHub, a clear README with screenshots, and one or two lines on the problem it solves and your role.
  • A short about section, a way to contact you, and your CV linked.
  • A real outreach push: send your portfolio to five actual opportunities (the business owner you have been building for, a job listing, an Upwork or Fiverr profile, a LinkedIn post, a local company).

Done when

  • The portfolio is live on a public URL and works on mobile.
  • All three projects load, with working links and clean READMEs.
  • Someone who has never met you can understand what you build in under a minute.
  • You have sent it to at least five real people or listings, and logged the responses in your journal.

Stretch goal: Record a 60-second Loom walking through your best project, and turn the client site you built into a real, paid first job.

Start lesson 20.1 →

docs.title: 20.1 Planning a portfolio that gets you hired
docs.text: You finished the course and built real projects. Now you need a place to show them. A portfolio is your home on the web. It tells people who you are and what you can build. This lesson helps you plan it before you write a single line.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• What a strong junior frontend portfolio looks like in 2026.
  • The 5 sections every good portfolio site needs.
  • How to write about yourself in a plain, warm voice.
  • How to pick the right 3 projects to feature.
  • Why a short writeup matters as much as a live link.
  • How to plan before you build, so you save time later.

docs.title: What a strong portfolio looks like in 2026
docs.text:
A good junior portfolio is small and clean. It loads fast. It shows a few real projects with live links. Each project has a short writeup next to it.

You do not need ten projects. You need three good ones. Quality wins over a long list. A hiring manager spends about a minute on your site. Make that minute count.

Your site should also be easy to read on a phone. Most people will open it on mobile first. If it looks broken on a small screen, you lose them.

Why does a portfolio matter so much? Because a CV is a list of claims. A portfolio is proof. A hiring manager can click your live link and see the result in ten seconds. That proof is worth more than any bullet point.

docs.title: The 5 sections of a portfolio site
docs.text:
Keep the structure simple. Five clear sections do the job:

  1. Hero. Your name and one line on what you do. For example, "I build clean, fast web apps."
  2. About. A short, human paragraph about you. Three or four sentences is enough.
  3. Projects. Your best 3 projects. Each one has a live link and a short writeup.
  4. Contact. An easy way to reach you. An email and a couple of links work fine.
  5. Footer. Quick links to your GitHub, LinkedIn, and email.

That is the whole site. Do not add more until you have a reason.

Here is how those sections stack up visually on a single page:

A <abbr title="A personal website that shows your projects and skills to employers. (Roman Urdu: aap ke kaam aur projects dikhane wali website)">portfolio</abbr> page split into five stacked sections: Hero at the top, then About, then Projects, then Contact, then Footer at the bottom. Hero Your name + one line + call to action About Short warm paragraph about you Projects Project 1 Project 2 Project 3 Contact Email + social links Footer GitHub · LinkedIn · email

A portfolio page is one long column of five clear sections. A visitor scrolls from Hero down to Footer in under a minute.

docs.title: What hiring managers look for in 30 seconds
docs.text:
Most hiring managers do not read. They scan. They spend the first 30 seconds deciding whether to keep looking or click away. Knowing what they scan for lets you put the right things in the right places.

What they look for Where they look Why it matters Who you are and what you build Hero section First line they read; must be instant and clear Live work, not just code Project live links Seeing the real thing beats reading code A real person, not a robot About paragraph Warm, honest writing builds quick trust Stack they recognise Tech tags on each project Shows you speak the same tools they use Mobile friendliness of the portfolio itself Their phone Broken layout = poor first impression How you think, not just what you shipped Project writeups Proves you can solve problems, not just follow tutorials Easy way to contact you Contact section Removes friction; if it is hard to reach you, they move on

Keep this table in mind when you plan each section. Every choice you make should serve one of those seven things.

docs.title: How to write about yourself
docs.text:
Many students freeze on the About section. They copy a stiff CV. Do not do that.

Write in plain, warm words. Use first person. Say "I build" and "I enjoy." Skip the buzzwords. Words like "passionate synergy expert" mean nothing.

Tell people what you build and who you help. For example, "I build web apps for small shops in Pakistan." That is clear and human. It sounds like a real person, not a robot reading a list.

Here is a quick test. Read your About section out loud. If you would never say those words to someone at a chai stall, rewrite it.

docs.title: Choosing 3 projects to feature
docs.text:
Pick finished projects that are live on the web. A deployed project beats a half-done demo every time.

Choose three that show range. A good mix could be:

  • Your ecommerce capstone, to show a full app.
  • The donation app, to show forms and data.
  • One personal idea, to show your own taste.

Depth beats a long list. One project you can explain well is worth more than five you cannot. Cut anything you are not proud of.

[CURATED LIST: Pakistani developer portfolio examples to be added here by the course author.]

Tip

Feature 3 finished, deployed projects. Do not show 10 half-built demos. Depth wins. A small, strong set of work tells a clearer story than a big messy pile.

docs.title: The role of process writeups
docs.text:
A live link shows what you built. A writeup shows how you think. Add a short story next to each project.

Keep each writeup to a few short lines. Answer four questions:

  • What problem did this project solve?
  • What choices did you make, and why?
  • What part was hard?
  • What did you learn from it?

This is the part hiring managers care about most. Anyone can copy a tutorial. They want to see how you solve problems. Your writeup proves you can think on your own.

Did you know

Many hiring managers read your project writeups before they open the code. A clear story about your choices can earn an interview on its own.

docs.title: Planning your sections: a checklist
docs.text:
Use this table before you write a single line of code. Fill each row on paper or in a note file. If you cannot fill a row, you are not ready to build that section yet.

Section What to plan Done? Hero Your full name; your one-line description of what you build [ ] About 3 to 4 warm, honest sentences; what you build, who you help, what you want next [ ] Project 1 Title; live URL; tech stack; problem it solved; what was hard [ ] Project 2 Title; live URL; tech stack; problem it solved; what was hard [ ] Project 3 Title; live URL; tech stack; problem it solved; what was hard [ ] Contact Email address; GitHub link; LinkedIn link [ ] Footer Same links as Contact; the year [ ] اردو میں مزید وضاحت

پراسیس رائٹ اپ کا مطلب ہے کہ آپ ہر پراجیکٹ کے ساتھ ایک چھوٹی سی کہانی لکھیں۔ اس میں بتائیں کہ مسئلہ کیا تھا اور آپ نے کیا فیصلے کیے۔ یہ بھی لکھیں کہ کون سا حصہ مشکل تھا اور آپ نے کیا سیکھا۔ ملازمت دینے والے لوگ یہ دیکھنا چاہتے ہیں کہ آپ سوچتے کیسے ہیں۔ صرف لائیو لنک کافی نہیں، آپ کی سوچ سب سے اہم ہے۔ پورٹ فولیو پلاننگ کا مطلب یہ ہے کہ پہلے کاغذ پر لکھیں، پھر کوڈ کریں۔ اس طرح وقت بچتا ہے اور نتیجہ بہتر ہوتا ہے۔

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Open a notebook or a blank file and plan your portfolio on paper before you code. List the 5 sections, then write your one-line hero text and a four-sentence About paragraph in plain, warm words. Pick the 3 projects you will feature and, for each one, jot down quick answers to the four writeup questions: what problem it solved, what choices you made, what was hard, and what you learned. Use the planning checklist table above to make sure you have everything before you open your code editor.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. What are the 5 sections of a portfolio site?
  2. Why is it better to show 3 projects instead of 10?
  3. What four questions should a project writeup answer?
  4. Why do hiring managers care about writeups, not just live links?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You now have a clear plan for your portfolio. You know the sections, the voice, and the projects. Next you will turn that plan into a real, live site.

Next lesson: 19.2 Build your portfolio →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • freeCodeCamp: How to build a developer portfolio website
  • The Odin Project

docs.title: 20.2 Build your portfolio
docs.text: In 19.1 you planned your portfolio. Now you build it. You will use the same stack you learned in this course, so nothing here is new. You just put the pieces together into one site that shows who you are.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• How to build each portfolio section with Next.js and Tailwind.
  • How to reuse one project card component for every project.
  • How to add light motion with Framer Motion or GSAP.
  • What MDX is and how to write project case studies with it.
  • How to make a contact form that sends real email with Resend.
  • How to keep the site fast and respect reduced motion.

docs.title: The stack you already know
docs.text:
You do not need new tools. Build the portfolio with this stack:

  • Next.js + Tailwind for the pages and styling.
  • Framer Motion or GSAP for small, tasteful motion.
  • React Three Fiber for one optional 3D touch, only if it fits.

Pick one motion library, not both. Keep the 3D touch small, like a slow rotating shape in the hero. A heavy 3D scene slows the site and hurts you. Start a fresh Next.js project.

npx create-next-app@latest my-portfolio
cd my-portfolio
npm run dev

This makes a new app and starts the dev server. Open the local URL in your browser. You now have a blank page to build on.

docs.title: Portfolio sections checklist
docs.text:
Before you start coding each section, use this table. It tells you what belongs in each piece, what to skip, and the common mistake that trips up beginners.

Section Must have Skip Common mistake Hero Name, one-line role, one CTA button Long bio, skills list Too much text; hero should be 5 seconds to read About 3 to 5 warm sentences, current goal Job history, buzzwords Stiff CV language; write like you talk Projects Image, title, one-line problem, tech tags, live link, repo link Half-built demos Too many projects; three strong ones beat ten weak ones Contact Email, GitHub, LinkedIn Long contact form with many fields Making it hard to reach you; keep it simple Footer Name, year, 3 quick links Excessive navigation Copying the full header nav; footer should be light

docs.title: The anatomy of a great project card
docs.text:
A project card is the main unit of your Projects section. Every detail on it has a purpose. Here is what a strong card contains and why each part is there:

Anatomy of a portfolio <abbr title="One reusable component that shows a single project on your portfolio. (Roman Urdu: ek reusable component jo aik project dikhata hai)">project card</abbr>: screenshot at top, then title, one-line problem statement, tech tags row, and two buttons at the bottom for live link and repo link. Screenshot or preview image (shows the real app; no placeholder art) Weather app for Pakistani cities Lets a user search any city and see live temperature React Tailwind Next.js Vercel Live link GitHub repo 1. Screenshot 2. Title 3. One-line problem 4. Tech tags 5. Live + repo buttons

Every element on a project card has a purpose: the screenshot lets a reviewer see the work instantly; the one-line problem shows you understood the goal; tech tags let them know your tools; the two buttons give easy next steps.

docs.title: The hero
docs.text:
The hero is the first thing people see. Keep it simple. Show your name, one line on what you do, and one call to action.

export default function Hero() {
  return (
    <section className="flex min-h-screen flex-col justify-center px-6">
      <h1 className="text-5xl font-bold">Ayesha Khan</h1>
      <p className="mt-4 text-xl text-gray-600">
        I build fast, accessible websites with React and Next.js.
      </p>
      <a
        href="#projects"
        className="mt-8 inline-block rounded bg-black px-6 py-3 text-white"
      >
        See my work
      </a>
    </section>
  );
}

Your name is big. One line says what you do. The button sends people to your projects. That is all a hero needs.

Warning

Keep hero motion light and respect prefers-reduced-motion. A flashy portfolio that lags on a phone loses you the job. Most hiring managers open your site on their phone first. Test it there.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
The about section is a short, warm paragraph. Write like you talk. Say what you enjoy and what you want to do next. Keep it to four or five sentences.

export default function About() {
  return (
    <section className="px-6 py-20">
      <h2 className="text-3xl font-bold">About me</h2>
      <p className="mt-4 max-w-2xl text-gray-700">
        I am a front-end developer from Karachi. I love turning designs into
        clean, fast pages. I am always learning new tools and ideas. Right now
        I am looking for my first full-time role.
      </p>
    </section>
  );
}

Notice the line about always learning. That is honest and it matters.

A note on talab al-ilm

This course ends, but learning does not. The web changes every year, and the best developers keep studying. Talab al-ilm, the seeking of knowledge, is a lifelong path. Say in your About section that you are always learning. It is true, and employers respect it.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
Your projects are the heart of the portfolio. Build one card component and reuse it for every project. This is the same idea as the ProductCard from Chapter 10.

export default function ProjectCard({ project }) {
  return (
    <article className="rounded-lg border p-4">
      <img
        src={project.image}
        alt={project.title}
        className="rounded"
      />
      <h3 className="mt-4 text-xl font-semibold">{project.title}</h3>
      <p className="mt-2 text-sm text-gray-600">{project.problem}</p>
      <ul className="mt-2 flex flex-wrap gap-2">
        {project.stack.map((tag) => (
          <li key={tag} className="rounded bg-gray-100 px-2 py-1 text-sm">
            {tag}
          </li>
        ))}
      </ul>
      <div className="mt-4 flex gap-4">
        <a href={project.live} className="underline">Live link</a>
        <a href={project.repo} className="underline">Repo</a>
      </div>
    </article>
  );
}

Each card shows an image, a title, a one-line problem statement (Roman Urdu: masla kya tha), the stack tags, a live link, and a repo link. You pass in one project object and the card draws itself. Then you map over your projects list to show them all.

Tip

Reuse one ProjectCard component for every project, just like the ProductCard from Chapter 10. If you want to change the card style later, you edit one file and every project updates.

docs.title: MDX for project case studies
docs.text:
A simple card is good, but a case study tells the full story. For that, use MDX. MDX is Markdown plus React components. So you write your project writeup in plain Markdown, then drop in live components where you want them.

Next.js supports MDX. You can show a working demo button right next to your text. Here is a small MDX file.

# How I built the weather app

I started with the API and a simple fetch. Here is a live demo
you can click right inside the writeup:

<WeatherDemo />

The hardest part was caching the results to keep it fast.

The # heading and the paragraphs are normal Markdown. The <WeatherDemo /> is a real React component. This lets you explain your work and let people try it at the same time.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ایم ڈی ایکس کا مطلب ہے مارک ڈاؤن کے ساتھ ری ایکٹ کمپوننٹ۔ آپ اپنے پراجیکٹ کی کہانی عام مارک ڈاؤن میں لکھتے ہیں۔ پھر جہاں چاہیں وہاں ایک زندہ ری ایکٹ کمپوننٹ شامل کر دیتے ہیں۔ اس طرح لوگ آپ کا کام پڑھ بھی سکتے ہیں اور چلا کر بھی دیکھ سکتے ہیں۔ نیکسٹ جے ایس اس کو آسانی سے سپورٹ کرتا ہے۔ پراجیکٹ کارڈ میں ایک لائن کا مسئلہ لکھنا بہت ضروری ہے، اس سے دیکھنے والے کو فوری سمجھ آتا ہے کہ آپ نے کیا بنایا اور کیوں بنایا۔

docs.title: Contact form with Resend
docs.text:
A contact form is useless if the messages never reach you. Use Resend to send real email. Resend has a free tier. You write a server action or API route that takes the form and emails it to you.

First install the package.

npm install resend

This adds the Resend library to your project. Next, write the handler. Here is a sketch of an API route.

import { Resend } from "resend";

const resend = new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY);

export async function POST(request) {
  const { name, email, message } = await request.json();

  await resend.emails.send({
    from: "portfolio@yourdomain.com",
    to: "you@example.com",
    subject: `New message from ${name}`,
    text: `${message} (reply to ${email})`,
  });

  return Response.json({ ok: true });
}

The route reads the name, email, and message from the form. Then it calls Resend to send you an email. Keep your API key in an environment variable, never in the code. Your form on the page posts to this route.

docs.title: Footer and speed
docs.text:
The footer is small. Add your name, the year, and links to your GitHub and LinkedIn. That is enough.

Now think about speed. Recall Chapter 19. Compress your images. Lazy load anything heavy. Keep motion light and short. Respect prefers-reduced-motion, which you learned in 11.4, so people who turn off animation get a calm page.

import { useReducedMotion } from "framer-motion";

function FadeIn({ children }) {
  const reduce = useReducedMotion();
  return (
    <motion.div
      initial={reduce ? false : { opacity: 0 }}
      animate={{ opacity: 1 }}
    >
      {children}
    </motion.div>
  );
}

When reduce is true, the element just appears with no fade. This respects the user setting and keeps the site kind to everyone.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Start a fresh Next.js project with create-next-app and build the hero and projects sections. Write a single ProjectCard component that takes a project object, then map over an array of your 3 real projects to render them all. Make sure each card has a problem field with one clear sentence about what the project solves. Style it with Tailwind and check that it looks good on a narrow phone width. If you add any motion, wire up useReducedMotion so the page stays calm for people who turn animation off.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. Why do you build one ProjectCard component instead of writing each card by hand?
  2. What is MDX, and what does it let you add to a Markdown writeup?
  3. Why do you keep your Resend API key in an environment variable?
  4. What does respecting prefers-reduced-motion do for your visitors?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Your portfolio works, but it is not done. Next you polish the small details and write a README that makes people want to hire you. Good polish is what turns a fine portfolio into one that wins interviews.

Next lesson: 19.3 Polish and the README that wins interviews →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Next.js: MDX
  • Resend: Next.js

docs.title: 20.3 Polish and the README that wins interviews
docs.text: You built your portfolio in 19.2. Now you make it speak for you. Most reviewers spend less than a minute on each project. A clear README and a short demo do the talking when you are not in the room. This lesson shows you how to polish both.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• The five questions every good README answers.
  • How to write a README section by section.
  • How to record a 60-second Loom demo for each project.
  • How to pin your six best repos on GitHub.
  • How to run a final polish pass before you share links.
  • A full README template you can copy and reuse.

docs.title: The five questions a README must answer
docs.text:
A README is the text file that shows on a repo's home page. Reviewers read it first. A good one answers five clear questions.

  1. What is it? One line that says what the app does.
  2. Why did you build it? Show the reason or the problem it solves.
  3. What is the stack? List the tools and languages you used.
  4. What is the live URL? Give a link they can click right now.
  5. What was hard about it? Explain one real challenge you solved.

The last question matters most. Anyone can list features. Telling a reviewer what was hard, and how you fixed it, shows how you think. That builds trust.

Tip

The "what was hard" section is what makes a reviewer trust you. Do not skip it. It turns a plain project into proof that you can solve real problems.

docs.title: What a README that wins interviews contains
docs.text:
Use this table to plan each section before you write it. Every part has a clear purpose. Skip a part and you leave a gap the reviewer notices.

Section What to write Why it matters Title Project name in one short phrase First thing the eye lands on; must be memorable One-line description What the app does, for whom Answers "should I keep reading?" in two seconds Live demo link Deployed URL, near the top Reviewers click before they read; make it easy Screenshot or GIF One image of the real, working UI Shows the result without asking them to run anything Why I built this One to three sentences on the problem Shows you build for a reason, not just to practice syntax Stack Bullet list of main tools Lets the reader judge your technical choices at a glance Features Three to five bullet points of what a user can do Shows product thinking, not just code Run locally Exact copy-paste commands: clone, install, dev Proves the project is real and respects the reader's time What I learned One honest paragraph on the hardest part The part reviewers remember; shows how you think

The live demo link at the top of the table is not an accident. Put it high on the page. If the reviewer can click it in three seconds, some of them will. If they have to scroll to find it, most will not.

docs.title: A perfect README, section by section
docs.text:
Walk through a strong README one piece at a time. Each piece has a job.

  • Title. The project name, short and clear.
  • One-line description. What it does, in one sentence.
  • Live demo link. The deployed URL at the top, easy to find.
  • A screenshot or GIF. One image so they see it without clicking.
  • The stack. The main tools, like React, Vite, and Tailwind.
  • Features. A short bullet list of what users can do.
  • How to run locally. The exact commands to start it.
  • What you learned. One honest paragraph on the hard part.

Keep each section short. A reviewer scans, so use headings and bullets. White space is your friend.

docs.title: A README template you can copy
docs.text:
Here is a full template. Copy it into a file named README.md at the root of each repo. Replace the bracket text with your own words.

# Project Name

A weather app that shows the live forecast for any Pakistani city.

## Live demo

https://your-project.vercel.app

## Screenshot

![App home screen](docs/screenshot.png)

## Why I built this

I wanted to practice fetching live data and showing it in a clean UI.
The goal was a fast, mobile-first app that loads in under two seconds.

## Stack

- React and Vite
- Tailwind CSS
- OpenWeather API
- Deployed on Vercel

## Features

- Search any city by name
- Shows temperature, humidity, and wind
- Saves your last searched city
- Works on mobile and desktop

## Run it locally

git clone https://github.com/your-name/your-project.git
cd your-project
npm install
npm run dev

## What I learned

The hardest part was handling the loading and error states.
At first the screen flashed blank while data loaded.
I added a loading spinner and a clear error message.
Now the app feels stable even on a slow connection.

Notice the live link sits near the top. Notice the "what I learned" part is honest and specific. That is the part reviewers remember.

docs.title: Record a 60-second Loom demo
docs.text:
A Loom demo is a short screen recording. You click through your app and explain it out loud. Loom is a free tool that records your screen and your voice.

Many reviewers will not run your code. They are busy. But they will watch a one-minute video. So give them one.

Keep it to 60 seconds. The five stages below form a clear structure. Each stage has one job.

A 60-second <abbr title="A free tool that records your screen and voice to make a short demo video. (Roman Urdu: screen record karne wala muft tool)">Loom</abbr> walkthrough divided into five labelled segments: problem (0-10s), live <abbr title="A short walkthrough that shows your app working. (Roman Urdu: chhoti video jo app chalti hui dikhaye)">demo</abbr> (10-40s), tech used (40-50s), your role (50-55s), what you would improve (55-60s). Problem Live demo Tech used Your role What you would improve 0-10 s 10-40 s 40-50 s 50-55 s 55-60 s

A 60-second Loom has five stages. The live demo gets the most time because that is what a reviewer actually wants to see.

Here is a simple script for each stage.

  1. Problem (0-10 s). Say the project name and the problem it solves. One sentence.
  2. Live demo (10-40 s). Click through the main feature live. Show the real app working.
  3. Tech used (40-50 s). Name the main tools you used and why you chose them.
  4. Your role (50-55 s). If it was a team project, say exactly what part you built.
  5. What you would improve (55-60 s). Name one honest thing you would change next time.

Add the Loom link near the top of your README, next to the live demo. Speak slowly and clearly. You do not need a perfect voice, just a calm one.

Did you know

Many reviewers watch a 60-second demo before they ever clone your repo. A short video can do more for you than a long README.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

ایک اچھا README وہ ہوتا ہے جو پانچ سوالوں کا واضح جواب دے: یہ کیا ہے، آپ نے کیوں بنایا، کون سے ٹولز استعمال کیے، لائیو لنک کیا ہے، اور سب سے مشکل حصہ کیا تھا۔ آخری سوال سب سے اہم ہے، کیونکہ یہ بتاتا ہے کہ آپ مسائل کیسے حل کرتے ہیں۔ اوپر والی ٹیبل میں ہر حصے کا کام بتایا گیا ہے تاکہ آپ لکھنے سے پہلے منصوبہ بنا سکیں۔ لائیو لنک ہمیشہ اوپر رکھیں، یعنی صفحے کے شروع میں، تاکہ ریویور فوری کلک کر سکے۔ Loom ویڈیو بھی بہت ضروری ہے کیونکہ زیادہ تر ریویور آپ کا کوڈ نہیں چلاتے، لیکن ایک منٹ کی ویڈیو ضرور دیکھتے ہیں۔ پانچ مراحل یاد رکھیں: مسئلہ، لائیو ڈیمو، ٹیکنالوجی، آپ کا کردار، اور آپ اگلی بار کیا بہتر کرتے۔

docs.title: Pin your six best repos
docs.text:
GitHub lets you pin up to six repos on your profile page. These pinned repos are your shop window. They are the first thing a recruiter sees.

Pin your strongest work only. Each pinned repo should have a clean README and a live link. Do not pin a half-finished project. Quality beats quantity here.

To pin a repo, go to your GitHub profile. Find the "Customize your pins" link. Tick your six best, then save. Put your most impressive project first.

docs.title: The final polish pass
docs.text:
Before you share any link, do one careful pass over each project. Small bugs cost you trust. Here is the checklist.

  • Consistent naming. Use clear, similar names across files and repos.
  • No leftover console.log. Remove every debug line from your code.
  • No dead links. Click every link and button. Fix anything broken.
  • Mobile works. Open the live site on your phone and test it.
  • Lighthouse is green. Run the audit from Chapter 19 and fix red scores.

Recall Chapter 19, where you ran Lighthouse for performance and accessibility. Run it again on the deployed site. Green scores tell a reviewer you care about quality.

A clean project says you finish your work. That is the message you want to send.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Pick your strongest project and write a real README.md for it using the template above. Make sure it answers all five questions, with the live link near the top and an honest "what I learned" paragraph at the end. Then record a 60-second Loom demo following the five-stage timeline, and add that link next to the live demo. Finish with the polish pass: remove every console.log, click each link to catch dead ones, and pin the repo on your GitHub profile.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. What are the five questions a good README must answer?
  2. Why does the "what was hard" section matter most to a reviewer?
  3. Why record a 60-second Loom demo instead of just sharing the code?
  4. How many repos can you pin on your GitHub profile, and how should you choose them?

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
Your portfolio now looks sharp and reads clearly. The next step is earning from your skills. In 19.4 you will learn how to find your first clients and freelance for halal income in Pakistan.

Next lesson: 19.4 First clients and freelancing in Pakistan →

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • Make a README
  • Pinning items to your profile (GitHub)

docs.title: 20.4 First clients and freelancing in Pakistan
docs.text: You have learned to build real websites. Now you can earn from this skill. Your first client is closer than you think, often right in your own town. This last lesson shows you how to find that client and how to do the work with honesty.

docs.title: What you'll know by the end
docs.text:
• How to find your first local clients near you
  • How to start with international clients on Upwork, Fiverr, and LinkedIn
  • How to price your work in PKR and in USD
  • How to agree scope, send an invoice, and take a deposit
  • How to keep your client work on the halal path
  • How to receive money from abroad and how to keep learning

docs.title: Start with local clients
docs.text:
Your first clients are often nearby. Look around your own town and your own contacts.

Many small groups need simple websites. Think about these:

  • Your local mosque
  • A charity like Alkhidmat
  • A small shop or restaurant near your home
  • A school or a coaching academy
  • A family business run by relatives

These groups rarely need a fancy app. They need a clean page that shows who they are, what they do, and how to reach them. You already know how to build that.

Local clients trust word of mouth (Roman Urdu: zubaan se zubaan tak). One happy shop owner will tell another. So your first job leads to your second job, and the chain grows.

Your first project may pay little, or you may build it free for a good cause. That is fine when you start. You gain a real reference and a real piece for your portfolio. Both are worth more than a small fee at this stage.

Tip

Your first local project, even a free one for a mosque or a charity, gives you a real reference and a portfolio piece. People can see your work, and you can show it to the next client. That proof opens the next door.

docs.title: Then reach international clients
docs.text:
Once you have one or two projects to show, you can look abroad. Clients overseas often pay in dollars, which goes far in Pakistan.

Three common places to start:

  • Upwork. You make a profile, then send proposals to posted jobs.
  • Fiverr. You list a service, called a gig (Roman Urdu: apni service ka aik package), and buyers come to you.
  • LinkedIn. You can send a short, polite message to a business owner.

Start small. Take simple jobs first, like a one page site or a small fix. Do them well and on time. Then ask the client for an honest review.

Reviews are your reputation (Roman Urdu: naik naam) online. A new freelancer with five good reviews wins more work than one with none. So protect your reviews by doing careful work.

Keep your LinkedIn messages short and respectful. Tell the person who you are, what you noticed about their business, and how you can help. Never spam. A polite message that adds value beats ten copy and paste ones.

docs.title: Freelance platforms compared
docs.text:
Choosing where to find work matters. Each platform works differently and suits different situations. This table gives you a clear comparison so you can pick the right starting point.

Platform Getting started Competition Payment Best for Upwork Create a profile, write proposals for job posts; first 10 proposals are free each month High globally, but mid-level for simple web work Hourly or fixed, via Upwork; Payoneer or direct deposit Developers who can write a strong proposal and want recurring clients Fiverr Create gigs describing your service; buyers search and come to you Very high for basic gigs; less for specific niches Fixed per order, via Fiverr; Payoneer withdrawal Beginners offering a clear, packaged service like "landing page in 5 days" LinkedIn Polish your profile, then message business owners directly; no bidding Low for direct outreach; most freelancers do not do this You invoice the client directly; Wise or bank transfer Developers comfortable with a short outreach message to a real business Local clients Word of mouth, WhatsApp, walking into a shop Very low; most have no developer contact at all Cash or local bank transfer in rupees Your very first project; builds confidence and a real reference fast

The table shows a pattern. The platforms with less competition, LinkedIn and local clients, require you to reach out yourself. The ones where clients come to you, Fiverr, have more noise. Both are valid. Start where you feel you can actually take action today.

docs.title: Pricing in PKR and in USD
docs.text:
Pricing confuses most new freelancers. Keep it simple at the start.

Local clients pay in rupees. They often expect lower prices, because that is the local market. A small shop site might be a few thousand rupees, or free for a cause.

International clients pay in dollars. The same work can earn much more, because their market is different. This is normal and fair.

When you start, charge by the project, not by the hour. You are still slow, so hourly pricing punishes you. A fixed project price is clear for both sides. The client knows the cost, and you know the goal.

As your portfolio grows, raise your rates. Each finished project makes you faster and more trusted. Higher rates follow real proof, not wishful thinking.

اردو میں مزید وضاحت

شروع میں قیمت طے کرنا مشکل لگتا ہے، لیکن اسے آسان رکھیں۔ مقامی گاہک روپے میں ادائیگی کرتے ہیں اور عام طور پر کم قیمت کی توقع رکھتے ہیں۔ بین الاقوامی گاہک ڈالر میں ادائیگی کرتے ہیں، اس لیے وہی کام زیادہ کما سکتا ہے۔ ابتدا میں گھنٹے کے بجائے پورے پراجیکٹ کی ایک طے شدہ قیمت لیں۔ جیسے جیسے آپ کا کام اور تجربہ بڑھے، اپنی قیمتیں بڑھاتے جائیں۔ اوپر والی ٹیبل میں چاروں راستوں کا موازنہ ہے تاکہ آپ اپنے لیے صحیح شروعات کا انتخاب کر سکیں۔ فنل والے خاکے سے یہ سمجھنا آسان ہو جاتا ہے کہ پہلا کام ملنے سے پہلے بہت سارے لوگوں تک پہنچنا پڑتا ہے، اس لیے ہمت نہ ہاریں۔

docs.title: The application funnel
docs.text:
Getting your first paid project feels slow at the start. That is normal. Think of it as a funnel (Roman Urdu: qaif). You reach many people, a few reply, fewer still get on a call with you, and one of them becomes a client. The numbers look rough at first and improve as your portfolio grows.

A narrowing <abbr title="The narrowing path from many outreach messages down to one client. (Roman Urdu: qaif, bahut se logon tak pahunchne se le kar ek kaam milne tak ka safar)">funnel</abbr> with four levels: reach out to many at the top, some reply, a few calls, first job at the bottom. Reach out to many Some reply A few calls First job 20+ messages 5 to 8 2 to 3 1

Every freelancer starts at the wide top of the funnel. The numbers narrow at each stage. That is not failure; it is how the process works. Keep sending messages and the bottom of the funnel fills.

The funnel shows why giving up after two or three messages is the wrong move. You are still at the very top. Keep going.

docs.title: Contracts and invoicing basics
docs.text:
You do not need a lawyer for your first jobs. You need clear writing.

Before you start, agree on three things with the client:

  • Scope. What exactly will you build, and what is not included.
  • Price. The full amount and the currency.
  • Timeline. When you will deliver the work.

Put these in writing, even a simple WhatsApp message. Writing protects both of you. It stops the slow creep where a small site quietly grows into ten pages for the same price.

Send a clear invoice (Roman Urdu: hisaab ki parchi). An invoice is a short document that lists the work, the price, and how to pay. It looks professional and keeps your records clean.

Take a deposit (Roman Urdu: peshgi) before you begin. A common split is half before and half on delivery. The deposit shows the client is serious. It also covers your time if plans change.

Warning

Always agree scope, price, and timeline in writing before you start, even a WhatsApp message counts. A spoken promise is easy to forget. A written line is easy to point to when memories differ.

docs.title: A first client, from Sahiwal
docs.text:
Let me tell you about my first paying client. His name does not matter, but the lesson does.

When I was starting out in Sahiwal, a cloth shop owner near my home needed a simple site. He had heard from a cousin that I "did computer work." He was not sure what a website even was. He just wanted people to find his shop and call him.

I was nervous. I quoted him a small price, took half as a deposit, and wrote the plan in a short message. Three pages. His products, his address, his phone number. Nothing more.

I built it in a week. He was so happy that he showed it to two other shopkeepers in the bazaar. Both of them called me within a month.

Here is what that small job taught me. The site was simple, but the trust was not small. I had done honest work, charged a fair price, and finished on time. That reputation, built one careful job at a time, carried me further than any clever code ever did.

docs.title: Staying on the halal path
docs.text:
Your skill can earn good money. Make sure that money stays clean.

Turn down work from haram industries. That includes:

  • Gambling and betting sites
  • Interest based lending, called riba
  • Alcohol and other haram products

Refuse deceptive work too. Some clients ask for tricks that fool people. Say no to:

  • Fake countdown timers that pressure buyers with a false rush
  • Hidden charges that appear only at the last step
  • Misleading claims about a product

Keep your own dealings honest as well. Promise only the scope you agreed. Send invoices that match the real work. Do not pad the bill.

Halal income (Roman Urdu: jaiz aur saaf kamai) is not only about the industry. It is about how you treat people in every step. Honest scope, honest prices, honest invoices. That path is slower sometimes, but it lasts.

A note on shukr

You now hold a skill that can feed a family and serve a community. That is a gift. Shukr, gratitude, means using it well: honest work, fair prices, and help for those who cannot pay. Skill is an amanah. Carry it with care.

docs.title: Getting paid from abroad
docs.text:
International clients pay in dollars, so you need a way to receive that money in Pakistan. A few common rails work well here:

  • Payoneer. Popular with Upwork and Fiverr freelancers. You get paid into a Payoneer account, then withdraw to your local bank.
  • Wise. Good for direct clients who want to send money to you. Low fees and clear rates.
  • Bank wire. A direct transfer from the client's bank to yours. Slower and pricier, but reliable for larger jobs.

The flow is usually the same. The client pays the platform or sends money to your service. The money lands in your Payoneer or Wise balance. You then move it to your Pakistani bank account in rupees.

Set this up early, before your first international payment. You do not want to scramble when the money is ready to arrive.

docs.title: Keep learning after this course
docs.text:
This course gives you a strong base. The real growth comes after, when you keep building and keep reading.

A few resources to return to often:

  • MDN Web Docs. The reference for HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Trust it.
  • javascript.info. Clear, deep lessons on JavaScript.
  • Official docs. When you use a tool or a library, read its own documentation first.

Beyond reading, do two things. Keep building projects, because building teaches what reading cannot. And read other people's code on GitHub, because you learn new patterns by seeing how others solve problems.

You will never know everything. No developer does. The skill is knowing how to find the answer and how to keep going.

docs.title: Try this
docs.text: Make a list of five local groups near you who could use a simple website, such as your mosque, a nearby shop, or a coaching academy. Pick one and write a short, honest message offering to build them a clean page, even free for a good cause. Before any work starts, draft the scope, price, and timeline in three lines you could send on WhatsApp, so you practise agreeing the deal in writing.

docs.title: Knowledge check
docs.text:
Don't write anything down. Just see if you can answer these in your head. If you can't, scroll back up. That's what this section is for.

  1. Name three kinds of local clients you could approach for your first project.
  2. Why should you charge by the project, not by the hour, when you start?
  3. What three things must you agree on in writing before you begin work?
  4. Name two types of work you should refuse to keep your income halal.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You reached the end of the book. Take a moment and feel that. You started not knowing what a tag was, and now you can build and ship real websites for real people.

The pages of this book taught you the ideas. The real learning begins now, in your own hands. Build projects. Break things. Fix them. Each one makes you stronger than any chapter could alone.

Keep shipping. Build for your mosque, your shop, your friend's idea, then build for clients. Keep reading and keep your skills sharp. When you forget a detail, come back to these lessons and use them as a reference. That is what they are here for.

You have a skill that can change your life and serve your community. Use it well, stay honest, and keep building. The work ahead is yours, and you are ready for it.

docs.title: Going deeper (optional)
docs.text:
These are for the curious. You don't need them to continue.

  • MDN Web Docs
